EDUCATE. INNOVATE. INSPIRE.

Schulich Elysia

Schulich Elysia started it’s design phase in 2016 and this catamaran style cruise car was completed in 2019. This design was implemented to improve battery cooling as well as increase aerodynamics. Elysia raced in the 2019 American Solar Challenge where it took first place in the Multi Occupant Vehicle Class.

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash.71066ce2.jpeg&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

© 2026 Calgary Solar Car

Schulich Helios

Schulich Helios, the current project in the making by the University
of Calgary Solar Car Team, embodies the future of renewable automotive technology.
This vehicle, capable of theoretically reaching speeds of 110 km/h, represents the
culmination of our intensified efforts. The team aims for excellence at the Formula
Sun Grand Prix in the summer of 2026, showcasing the incredible potential of solar-powered
vehicles. Be sure to stay updated with any new developments!

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash1.ede3ae46.png&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

Schulich Elysia

Schulich Elysia started it’s design phase in 2016 and this catamaran
style cruise car was completed in 2019. This design was implemented to improve
battery cooling as well as increase aerodynamics. The Elysia raced in the 2019
American Solar Challenge where it took first place in the Multi Occupant Vehicle Class.
In summer of 2025, Elysia will be retiring at its final race at the Formula Sun Grand
Prix in Bowling Green, Kentucky. Stay tuned for race updates!

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash2.663f9d6b.jpeg&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

Schulich Delta

The Schulich Delta, Canada's first cruiser-class solar car, was built
by the University of Calgary Solar Car Team from 2012 to 2015. It
raced in the 2013 Bridgestone World Solar Challenge, finishing 8th,
and competed in the 2015 Formula Sun Grand Prix, completing 84 laps
with a fastest lap of 5:33.886 to finish 9th. Officially retired in
2015, the Delta continues to inspire through educational
demonstrations.

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash3.66b82fe5.jpeg&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

Schulich Axiom

Schulich Axiom was the University of Calgary Solar Car Team's last
Challenger class car. In 2010, it finished 6th at the Formula Sun
Grand Prix (418.2 miles covered) and 6th again in the American Solar
Challenge (Broken Arrow, OK to Naperville, IL) with a time of
33:35.26, placing as the top Canadian team in both races. In 2011,
Axiom competed in the World Solar Challenge from Darwin to Adelaide,
covering 1,840 km and finishing 18th before being retired.

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash4.4c3061f4.jpeg&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

Schulich 1

The Schulich I debuted at the 2007 Panasonic World Solar Challenge,
finishing 8th and completing 2999 km in 51 hr, 43 min as the top
Canadian team. It later competed in the 2008 North American Solar
Challenge, finishing 6th—a record for the University of Calgary Solar
Car Team—completing the race in 75:42.53. As the team’s second car, it
introduced gallium arsenide solar cells, replacing the silicon cells
used in its predecessor.

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash5.5b2339b5.jpeg&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

Schulich Soleon

The University of Calgary Solar Car Team built Soleon in 2004 for the
inaugural North American Solar Challenge (2005), finishing 13th with a total driving
time of 73:55.13—an impressive debut. Soleon then competed in the 2005 World Solar
Challenge, placing 1st in its class and 10th overall. It was retired in 2006
to make way for Schulich I.

![backsplash](https://calgarysolarcar.ca/_next/image?url=%2F_next%2Fstatic%2Fmedia%2Fbacksplash6.b3bb60e5.jpeg&w=3840&q=75&dpl=dpl_8GCthmzvznJCTdEeqouXZThUH9cB)

Our Team

The University of Calgary Solar Car Team was established in 2004 and
has evolved to become an experienced solar racing team that also works
to educate the community on renewable energy. The team is composed of
students from various departments, working in collaboration with
faculty to develop sustainable energy solutions. We are currently
working on our sixth car and have completed 7 races with previous
vehicles.

# University of Calgary Solar Car Team University of Calgary Solar Car Team Solar car racing team at the University of Calgary [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/7/7f/UC_Solar_Team_Logo.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:UC_Solar_Team_Logo.jpg) University of Calgary Solar Team Logo The **University of Calgary Solar Car Team** is a multi-disciplinary student-run [solar car racing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car_racing "Solar car racing")("raycing") team at the [University of Calgary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Calgary "University of Calgary"), based in [Calgary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), [Alberta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), [Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Canada "Canada"). It was established to design and build a solar car to compete internationally in the [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") (ASC) (previously named the North American Solar Challenge) and the [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") (WSC). The team is primarily composed of undergraduate students studying Engineering, Business, Science, Arts and Kinesiology. The mission of the University of Calgary Solar Car Team is to educate the community about [sustainable energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_energy "Sustainable energy") and to serve as an interdisciplinary project through which students and faculty from various departments can collaborate in supporting sustainable energy. ## History The University of Calgary Solar Car Team was established by the University in the fall of 2004 in response to the North American Solar Challenge 2005 whose finish line was in Calgary, Alberta. The team's first vehicle was completed in 9 months and managed to place 13th despite their limited experience. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/63/UCSolarX1-StampedeParade-Wikipedia.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:UCSolarX1-StampedeParade-Wikipedia.jpg) This is an image of the X1, the prototype solar car created by the UC Solar team for testing purposes. In this image it is being piloted by driver James Snell for the [Calgary Stampede Parade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calgary_Stampede_Parade "Calgary Stampede Parade") in the summer of 2005. ## Soleon ### Miracle workers The team performed a "miracle" by managing to establish a strong student base with which to construct the car, procure significant sponsorship and successfully build a highly successful vehicle with limited experience all in approximately 9 months. Established teams have two years to refine existing designs between [races](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car_racing "Solar car racing"). ### The X1 (prototype) In preparation for its first rayce, the University of Calgary Solar Team constructed and tested a prototype of their designs before building the final car. The X1 was a mock-up of and predecessor to Soleon. The X1 was used for driver training and allowed the team to test various design decisions to help ensure a successful final product. The X1 was constructed from a steel chassis with a [fibreglass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibreglass "Fibreglass") shell which was coated with [gelcoat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gelcoat "Gelcoat") which made the vehicle approximately twice the final weight of its sister car. ### First race results In its first year of existence, the University of Calgary Solar Team successfully competed in the NASC and the WSC. In the 2005 NASC, Soleon, their first generation rayce car placed 13th out of 17 cars that made it to the finish line. In the 2005 WSC Soleon placed 10th overall (out of 18) and first in its class. After hearing about this success, [Seymour Schulich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seymour_Schulich "Seymour Schulich") was inspired to donate $25 million (another $25 million was matched by the Alberta government) to the University of Calgary Engineering department, which was renamed the [Schulich School of Engineering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schulich_School_of_Engineering "Schulich School of Engineering"). ## Schulich I ### 2007 WSC race Since the success of Soleon in the 2005 races, the team had to redesign for the new regulations for WSC, and prepare for the harder competition it faced from changing class to the higher, more competitive Challenge class. This class included higher efficiency [solar cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell"), upright seating, and teams that had been raycing for the last 20 years. After shipping their new car to Australia, and testing it before scrutineering, the car had a rear tire blow out on the race track in [Darwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory"), and resulted in the car spinning around having the tail section impacting into the guard rail and ripped from the car. The team then had to rebuild and redesign the tail section in one night before racing. Despite the higher competition and race track crash, the team managed to be the first (of six) Canadian teams to cross the timing finish line, finish 8th (out of 19) in the Challenge class, and 15th (out of 37) overall. ### 2008 NASC race Schulich I was improved after WSC 2007 for racing in the 2008 North American Solar Challenge. The rayce from Dallas, Texas to Calgary lasted ten days, with the University of Calgary Solar Team placing 6th (out of 15). ## Schulich Axiom ### 2010 ASC The University of Calgary Solar Car Team's 3rd generation car, Schulich Axiom, was first raced in the 2010 ASC. The race spanned 1770km from Tulsa, Oklahoma to Chicago, Illinois. The team finished in 6th place (of 18 teams) and received both the Sportsmanship and Mechanical Engineering awards for the race. With regulation changes for the 2011 WSC, the team re-engineered the design of Schulich Axiom and completely rebuilt the solar car. Some of the changes made include switching to silicon solar cells and reducing the weight of the car.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Calgary_Solar_Car_Team#cite_note-1) ### 2011 WSC The 2011 WSC took place on October 16–23, 2011 and was a road endurance race from Darwin, Northern Territory to Adelaide, South Australia, a total of over 3000km. The University of Calgary Solar Team entered the Schulich Axiom and placed ## Schulich Delta ### Design and Construction: Focused on Practicality Schulich Delta, the team's fourth generation vehicle, was designed over the course of eight months, and construction took another three months. Delta was a radical departure for the team, featuring two doors, cup holders, four wheels, a passenger seat and cargo space for the very first time. It was also Canada's first cruiser class car. ### 2013 WSC The team entered the Schulich Delta to compete in the Cruiser class in the 2013 World Solar Challenge that took place on October 6–13, 2013. The team placed 8th in the Cruiser Class. ### 2015 FSGP After some improvements to Delta's design, the team competed in the Formula Sun Grand Prix 2015 held at the [Circuit of the Americas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circuit_of_the_Americas "Circuit of the Americas") in Austin, Texas where it was the first cruiser-class vehicle to ever compete at the event. The event took place on Sunday, July 26 until Friday, July 31, 2016. The team completed 84 laps, placing 9th overall. Its fastest lap was 5:33.886. ## Schulich Elysia ### Design and construction The University of Calgary's sixth generation vehicle looked to make significant improvements of their cruiser class vehicle while keeping practicality their main focus. The team started the design phase in 2016 and crafted a [catamaran](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catamaran "Catamaran") inspired frame to help increase aerodynamics and reduce weight. Furthermore, the team implemented [NACA ducts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NACA_duct "NACA duct") and fans to greatly improve on battery cooling performance. Another main focus was on the "every-day" user by implementing a touch-screen centre console info-tainment system, cupholders, back up cameras as well as speakers. The team also implemented a sophisticated charging system that includes the electric vehicle standard [SAE-J1772 connector](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SAE_J1772 "SAE J1772") which allows the car to do level 1 or 2 type charging from the wall. ### 2019 FSGP After a 3-year build cycle, the University of Calgary Solar Car team raced the Schulich Elysia at the [Formula Sun Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix "Formula Sun Grand Prix") 2019 held at the [Circuit of the Americas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circuit_of_the_Americas "Circuit of the Americas") in [Austin, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"). This was the first year that the competition had a separate MOV (Multi-Occupancy Vehicle) category and the team finished first place in this class and fourth place overall out of 17 teams, completing 122 laps. Their fastest lap was 5:05.971. #### Awards At the FSGP 2019, the team was given four additional awards at the award ceremony: The Mechanical Design award, the MOV Charging System Award, Aesthetics Award and the Corner 8 Award. ## Cars [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/f6/UCSolar-X1-Wikipedia.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:UCSolar-X1-Wikipedia.JPG) ### X1 (prototype) **Maximum Achieved Speed:** ~70km/h **Solar Array Type:** none (stickers merely for show) **Chassis:** Steel [Space frame](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_frame "Space frame") **Shell Composition:** [Fibreglass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibreglass "Fibreglass") & [Gelcoat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gelcoat "Gelcoat") **Commissioned:** May 2005 **Decommissioned:** June 2006 **Current Uses:** X1 has been decommissioned. The chassis is all that remains. It is suspended against a wall in the University of Calgary Solar Team's workshop. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1a/Soleon_on_day_2_of_NASC_05.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Soleon_on_day_2_of_NASC_05.jpg) ### Soleon **Maximum Achieved Speed:** 140km/h **Solar Array Type:** Silicon **Chassis:** Aluminum [Space frame](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_frame "Space frame") **Shell Composition:** [Carbon Fiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Fiber "Carbon Fiber") & [Kevlar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar") **Weight:** ~500lbs **Commissioned:** June 2005 **Decommissioned:** July 2007 **Current Uses:** In the fall of 2008 Soleon was donated to the [Calgary Telus World of Science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Telus_World_of_Science,_Calgary "Telus World of Science, Calgary") and was on display for 2 years. Soleon is now retired and looking for a nice warm garage to rest. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/UC_Solar_Team_with_Schulich.JPG/960px-UC_Solar_Team_with_Schulich.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:UC_Solar_Team_with_Schulich.JPG) ### Schulich I **Maximum Achieved Speed:** 105km/h **Solar Array Type:** [Gallium arsenide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gallium_arsenide "Gallium arsenide") (GaAs) Triple-junction **Chassis:** Steel [Space frame](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space_frame "Space frame") **Shell Composition:** [Carbon Fiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Fiber "Carbon Fiber") & [Kevlar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar") **Weight:** ~520lbs **Commissioned:** September 2007 **Decommissioned:** May 2011 **Current Uses:** Participated in the 2007 [Panasonic World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Panasonic_World_Solar_Challenge "Panasonic World Solar Challenge") and the 2008 [North American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Solar_Challenge "North American Solar Challenge"). Since Axiom is now being used for driver training, mechanical testing and PR events, Schulich I has been retired. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/AxiomStartLine.jpg/960px-AxiomStartLine.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:AxiomStartLine.jpg) ### Schulich Axiom (2010) **Maximum Achieved Speed:** 130km/h **Solar Array Type:** [Gallium arsenide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gallium_arsenide "Gallium arsenide") (GaAs) Triple-junction **Chassis:** [Carbon Fiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Fiber "Carbon Fiber") & [Kevlar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar") **Shell Composition:** [Carbon Fiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Fiber "Carbon Fiber") & [Kevlar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar") **Weight:** ~600lbs **Commissioned:** October 2009 **Current Uses:** Placed 6th overall in the 2010 [North American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Solar_Challenge "North American Solar Challenge"). Schulich Axiom (2010) is now predominantly being used as a display vehicle. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d5/AxiomLight.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:AxiomLight.jpg) ### Schulich Axiom (2011) **Maximum Achieved Speed:** 110km/h **Solar Array Type:** Silicon Monocrystalline (Si) UC Solar Embedded **Chassis:** [Carbon Fiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Fiber "Carbon Fiber") **Shell Composition:** [Carbon Fiber](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Fiber "Carbon Fiber") **Weight:** ~390lbs **Commissioned:** May 2011 **Current Uses:** Planning on racing in the 2011 [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge"). All improvements have since been completed to Schulich Axiom. "It is evident from the previous race that weight is no minor detail. We have taken Axiom on its diet and the result is stunning. Axiom has dropped from around 600 lbs. without driver and ballast to 390 lbs. A loss of 210 lbs!" -Mico Madamesila ## References 1. American Solar Challenge

The **American Solar Challenge** (ASC), previously known as the **North American Solar Challenge** and **Sunrayce**, is a [solar car race](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car_racing "Solar car racing") across the United States. In the race, teams from colleges and universities throughout North America design, build, test, and race [solar-powered vehicles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_vehicle "Solar vehicle") in a long distance [road rally](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rallying "Rallying")-style event. ASC is a test of teamwork, engineering skill, and endurance that stretches across thousands of miles of public roads.

The competition occurs every two years in the summer of even years. ASC 2020 was postponed until 2021 due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-1)

## Format and organization

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Format and organization")\]

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/North_American_Solar_Challenge_2005.jpg/250px-North_American_Solar_Challenge_2005.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:North_American_Solar_Challenge_2005.jpg) Solar race cars and crews at the start of the 2005 race in Austin, Texas. _Photo: Stefano Paltera/North American Solar Challenge._

### Rules

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rules")\]

- Race consists of a series of timed stages between predetermined locations; all teams begin and end each stage in the same location
- The team with the lowest overall elapsed time wins
- The total area of all solar cells and related reflectors, etc. must not exceed 6 square meters
- When the vehicle has stopped, the solar array may be reoriented toward the sun for charging batteries
- Strict specifications and engineering scrutiny process is provided for vehicle configuration, safety requirements, and other standards
- Previous races have divided teams into **open** and **stock** classes based on levels of solar cell and battery technologies.
- The [Formula Sun Grand Prix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix "Formula Sun Grand Prix") track race serves as a qualifier for the more prestigious ASC.

## History

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")\]

Originally called _Sunrayce USA_, the first race was organized and sponsored by [General Motors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Motors_Corporation "General Motors Corporation") in 1990 in an effort to promote automotive engineering and solar energy among college students. At the time, GM had just won the inaugural [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") in Australia in 1987; rather than continue actively racing, it instead opted to sponsor collegiate events.

Subsequent races were held in 1993, 1995, 1997 and 1999 under the name _Sunrayce \[year\]_ (e.g. [Sunrayce 93](http://www.rose-hulman.edu/SolarPhantom/sunrayce93.html)). In 2001, the race was renamed [_American Solar Challenge_](http://www1.eere.energy.gov/solar/deployment.html) and was sponsored by the [United States Department of Energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy") and the [National Renewable Energy Laboratory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Renewable_Energy_Laboratory "National Renewable Energy Laboratory"). Beginning in 2005, its name changed again to _North American Solar Challenge_, in order to reflect the border crossing into Canada and the addition of co-sponsor [Natural Resources Canada](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_Resources_Canada "Natural Resources Canada"). The name was changed back to ASC in 2010.

After the 2005 race, the U.S. Department of Energy discontinued its sponsorship, resulting in no scheduled race for 2007. Sponsorship was taken over for NASC 2008 by [Toyota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Toyota "Toyota").[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-2) The **American Solar Challenge** is now governed by the [_Innovators Educational Foundation_](http://americansolarchallenge.org/about/innovators-educational-foundation/).

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Sunrunner.jpg/250px-Sunrunner.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sunrunner.jpg) University of Michigan's _Sunrunner_, winner of the inaugural Sunrayce USA in 1990.

### 1990

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1990")\]

The original, 1,800 mi (2,900 km) **Sunrayce USA** route started at [Disney World](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disney_World "Disney World") in Orlando, Florida and ended at the [General Motors Technical Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Motors_Technical_Center "General Motors Technical Center") in Warren, Michigan.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-3) The winner of the first race was the [University of Michigan Solar Car Team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car_Team "University of Michigan Solar Car Team")'s _Sunrunner_, with an average speed of 24.7 mph (39.8 km/h), followed by [Western Washington University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Washington_University "Western Washington University")'s _Viking XX_.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-4)

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GM_Sunrayce_USA "GM Sunrayce USA")

### 1993

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1993")\]

**Sunrayce 93** was held June 20–26, 1993. The race route covered over 1,100 miles (1,800 km) starting in [Arlington, TX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arlington,_TX "Arlington, TX") and ending in [Minneapolis, Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minneapolis,_Minnesota "Minneapolis, Minnesota").[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-5) The first place car was [_Maize & Blue_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1993_%22Maize_%26_Blue%22_University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car "1993 \"Maize & Blue\" University of Michigan Solar Car") from the [University of Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") followed by the _Intrepid_ from [Cal Poly Pomona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cal_Poly_Pomona "Cal Poly Pomona").

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunrayce_93 "Sunrayce 93")

### 1995

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1995")\]

**Sunrayce 95** ran along a 1,600-mile (2,600 km) route from Indianapolis, Indiana to Golden, Colorado. [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology")'s _Manta_ won the race with an average speed of 37.23 mph (59.92 km/h), followed by the [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project")'s _Aurora II_ just 18 minutes behind.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-6)

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunrayce_95 "Sunrayce 95")

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/SEIIITeam800H.jpg/250px-SEIIITeam800H.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:SEIIITeam800H.jpg) Cal State LA's _Solar Eagle III_, winner of Sunrayce USA in 1997.

### 1997

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1997")\]

**Sunrayce 1997** followed a familiar route from Indianapolis, Indiana to a finish line in Colorado Springs, Colorado.
[California State University-Los Angeles's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California_State_University_Los_Angeles "California State University Los Angeles") _Solar Eagle III_ won the nine-day **Sunrayce 97**.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-7) _Solar Eagle III_ averaged 43.29 mph (69.67 km/h), followed by [MIT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology")'s _Manta GT_ in second place.

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunrayce_97 "Sunrayce 97")

### 1999

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1999")\]

**Sunrayce 99**, running from Washington, D.C., to Orlando, Florida, was notable for its lack of sunshine. The [University of Missouri-Rolla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Missouri_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Missouri University of Science and Technology")'s _Solar Miner II_ won the race with an average speed of 25.3 mph (40.7 km/h).[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-8) The car from [Queen's University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston") placed second.

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunrayce_99 "Sunrayce 99")

### 2001

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2001")\]

In 2001, the race changed its name to the **American Solar Challenge** and followed a new route from Chicago, [Illinois](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to [Claremont, California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claremont,_California "Claremont, California") along much of the old [U.S. Route 66](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66").[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-9) The [University of Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") won the overall race and the Open Class with a total elapsed time of 56 hours, 10 minutes, and 46 seconds, followed by the [University of Missouri-Rolla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Missouri_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Missouri University of Science and Technology"). The [University of Arizona](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Arizona "University of Arizona") team won the Stock Class event.

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge_2001 "American Solar Challenge 2001")

### 2003

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2003")\]

The 2003 **American Solar Challenge** also followed [U.S. Route 66](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U.S._Route_66 "U.S. Route 66"). _Solar Miner IV_ from the [University of Missouri-Rolla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Missouri_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Missouri University of Science and Technology") won the race overall, as well as the Open Class, followed by the [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project")'s _Borealis II_.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-10) The Stock Class was won by the _Prairie Fire GT_ from [North Dakota State University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_Dakota_State_University "North Dakota State University").

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge_2003 "American Solar Challenge 2003")

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/BorderCrossingB3.jpg/250px-BorderCrossingB3.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:BorderCrossingB3.jpg) Minnesota's _Borealis III_ crosses the border to Canada on July 21, 2005.[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Stanfordsolarcar_solstice.jpg/250px-Stanfordsolarcar_solstice.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Stanfordsolarcar_solstice.jpg) Stanford's _Solstice_ team in 2005.

### 2005

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2005")\]

The 2005 race, renamed the **North American Solar Challenge**, was both the longest and most hotly contested race in the history of the event. The route covered 2,494.9 mi (4,015.2 km), taking the teams from [Austin, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") in the United States to [Calgary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), [Alberta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") in Canada. The race was won by the _Momentum_ from the [University of Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car_Team "University of Michigan Solar Car Team") with an average speed of 46.2 mph (74.4 km/h). The [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project")'s _Borealis III_ followed in second place less than 12 minutes behind, with an average speed of 46.0 mph (74.0 km/h).[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-11)[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-12) The lead teams often drove 65 mph (105 km/h) (the maximum allowed), but were slowed by rain in [Kansas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kansas "Kansas") and 20 mph (32 km/h) headwinds in Canada. [Stanford University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stanford_solar_car_project "Stanford solar car project")'s _Solstice_ won the Stock Class, followed in second place by the _Beam Machine_ from The [University of California, Berkeley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley").

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Solar_Challenge_2005 "North American Solar Challenge 2005")

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/81/Continuum_2008.jpg/250px-Continuum_2008.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Continuum_2008.jpg) Michigan's Continuum wins NASC 2008.

### 2008

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2008")\]

The 2008 **North American Solar Challenge** took place on July 13–22, 2008, mostly along the 2005 route from [Dallas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), [Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Texas "Texas") to [Calgary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"), [Alberta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"). The University of Michigan's _[Continuum](http://www.umsolar.com/)_ won the race with a total elapsed time of 51 hours, 41 minutes, and 53 seconds, marking that school's fifth victory. _Ra 7_ from [Principia College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principia_College "Principia College") followed in second place.[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-13)

As many of the top cars were bumping up against the 65 mph (105 km/h) race speed limit in the 2005 event, race rules were changed for 2008 order to improve safety and limit performance. Open class cars are now only allowed 6 square meters of active cell area, and upright seating is required for both open and stock class cars. The changes were carried over from the 2007 [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge").

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Solar_Challenge_2008 "North American Solar Challenge 2008")

### 2010

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2010")\]

The 2010 race, renamed the **American Solar Challenge**, ran June 20–26, 2010. The University of Michigan finished in first place, followed by the [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project")'s _Centaurus II_ in 2nd place and team Bochum from Germany in 3rd. The race route was entirely within the United States for the first time since 2003.[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-14)

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge_2010 "American Solar Challenge 2010")

### 2012

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: 2012")\]

Only four teams finished the 2012 **American Solar Challenge**, a 1600-mile race from Rochester, NY to St. Paul, MN, under solar power alone. The University of Michigan's _Quantum_ won the overall competition, over 10 hours ahead of the 2nd place team. The 2nd, 3rd, and 4th place teams were only an hour apart from each other. In order: [Iowa State University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University")'s _Hyperion_, [Principia College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principia_College "Principia College")'s _Ra7s_, and the [University of California, Berkeley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley")'s _Impulse_.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-15)

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge_2012 "American Solar Challenge 2012")

### 2014

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2014")\]

The 2014 **American Solar Challenge** reverted to the familiar south–north race route starting in [Austin, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), and finishing in [Minneapolis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), [Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"). The University of Michigan's _Quantum_ once again took 1st place, followed by [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project")'s _Centaurus III_. Both teams had brought back their cars from the 2012 event.[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-16)

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge_2014 "American Solar Challenge 2014")

### 2016

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2016")\]

The 2016 **American Solar Challenge** ran from [Brecksville, Ohio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brecksville,_Ohio "Brecksville, Ohio") to [Hot Springs, South Dakota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hot_Springs,_South_Dakota "Hot Springs, South Dakota") from July 30 to August 6, 2016. ASC partnered with the [National Park Service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), and the route included stages and checkpoints at 9 national parks, historic sites, or partner properties throughout the Midwest.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-17) The University of Michigan's _Aurum_ won the overall competition, by a margin of over 11 hours. In second place was the [Dunwoody College of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dunwoody_College_of_Technology "Dunwoody College of Technology") team in partnership with [Zurich University of Applied Sciences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zurich_University_of_Applied_Sciences "Zurich University of Applied Sciences"). The [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project")'s _Eos I_ made history as the first Cruiser Class vehicle to ever compete in ASC.

[Overall Standings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge_2016 "American Solar Challenge 2016")

### 2018

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2018")\]

The 2018 **American Solar Challenge** ran from [Omaha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omaha "Omaha"), [Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") to [Bend, Oregon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bend,_Oregon "Bend, Oregon") from July 14 to July 22, 2018. ASC partnered with the [National Park Service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), and the route included stages and checkpoints at historic sights along the Oregon Trail. It was the first to include a Cruiser Class, featuring more practical multi-occupant Solar Vehicles. [Western Sydney University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Sydney_University "Western Sydney University")'s car _UNLIMITED 2.0_ won the Challenger class competition by a margin of 16 minutes—the closest finish in ASC history—as The University of Michigan failed to defend their title with their car _Novum_.[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-18) The [University of Bologna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Bologna "University of Bologna") won the inaugural Cruiser class competition, and the [University of Waterloo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Waterloo "University of Waterloo") became the first Canadian Cruiser Class vehicle to ever compete in ASC.

### 2020

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2020")\]

The 2020 **American Solar Challenge** was postponed due to the COVID-19 pandemic. It was held in 2021, with the starting point at [Independence, Missouri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri") and the end point at [Las Vegas, New Mexico](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Las_Vegas,_New_Mexico "Las Vegas, New Mexico") from August 4 to August 7. The team from the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") took first place in the single-occupant class with _Nimbus_, with approximately 143 miles more in distance covered compared to second place, held by the [University of Kentucky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Kentucky "University of Kentucky") team. [Appalachian State](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Appalachian_State "Appalachian State") took first place in the multi-occupant vehicle class.

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/MIT_2022_ASC_Champion.jpg/250px-MIT_2022_ASC_Champion.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:MIT_2022_ASC_Champion.jpg) MIT posing with their back-to-back champion vehicle _Nimbus_.

### 2022

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2022")\]

The 2022 **American Solar Challenge** ran from [Independence, Missouri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri") to [Twin Falls, Idaho](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twin_Falls,_Idaho "Twin Falls, Idaho") from July 9 to July 16, 2022. Similar to the 2018 race, ASC partnered with the [National Park Service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), and the route traversed through historic sites and landmarks along the Oregon Trail. The team from the [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") took first place in the single-occupant class with their champion vehicle from the previous race, _Nimbus_. They finished the race with just over 73 miles more than second place finisher [Principia College](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principia_College "Principia College"). The winner of the Cruiser class was the [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"), with [Appalachian State University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Appalachian_State_University "Appalachian State University") coming in second place.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-19)

### 2024

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=American_Solar_Challenge&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2024")\]

[![The 2 Person Solar Vehicle Built by Students at Polytehnique Montreal Crosses the Electrek American Solar Challenge 2024 finish line as the checkered flag waves and team members run behind the car cheering for their accomplishment.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Poly_Montreal_Team_Crossing_ASC_2024_Finish_Line.jpg/250px-Poly_Montreal_Team_Crossing_ASC_2024_Finish_Line.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Poly_Montreal_Team_Crossing_ASC_2024_Finish_Line.jpg) Poly Montreal Crossing the ASC 2024 Finish Line

The Elecktrek **American Solar Challenge** 2024 ran from [Nashville, Tennessee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee") to [Casper, Wyoming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Casper,_Wyoming "Casper, Wyoming") from July 20 to July 27, 2024. Similar to the previous competitions, ASC partnered with the [National Park Service](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") this time following several [National Historic Trails](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Trails_System "National Trails System") and finishing at the [National Historic Trails Interpretive Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Historic_Trails_Interpretive_Center "National Historic Trails Interpretive Center").[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-20)

The Single Occupant Vehicle Class was won by the [University of Michigan Solar Car Team](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car_Team "University of Michigan Solar Car Team") with 2095.5 miles traveled during the event while the Multi-Occupant Vehicle Class was on by the team from [Polytechnique Montréal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytechnique_Montr%C3%A9al "Polytechnique Montréal") with a score of 73.86. [\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-21)

# World Solar Challenge World Solar Challenge Solar-powered car race event Motor race | [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/8/81/WorldSolarChallengeLogo.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:WorldSolarChallengeLogo.png) | | World Solar Challenge | | Venue | Stuart Highway | | Location | Australia | | Corporate [sponsor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sponsor_(commercial) "Sponsor (commercial)") | [Bridgestone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bridgestone "Bridgestone") | | First race | 1987 | | Distance | 3,022km (1,878mi) | | Duration | 4-7 days | | Most wins (team) | [Nuon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuna "Nuna") (7) (Challenger)
[Eindhoven](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Team_Eindhoven "Solar Team Eindhoven") (Cruiser) | Bridgestone World Solar Challenge[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Australia_ggc_route.png/960px-Australia_ggc_route.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Australia_ggc_route.png) 3,000km route of World Solar Challenge.[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Nuna3atZandvoort1.JPG/960px-Nuna3atZandvoort1.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Nuna3atZandvoort1.JPG) Nuna 3 of seven time victors, [Dutch](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") [Nuna](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuna "Nuna") team[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Solar_Car_Tokai_Challenger.JPG/960px-Solar_Car_Tokai_Challenger.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_Car_Tokai_Challenger.JPG) The winner of 2009 Global Green Challenge, "Tokai Challenger", Japan [Tokai University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokai_University "Tokai University") Solar Car Team The **World Solar Challenge** ( **WSC**), named the **Bridgestone World Solar Challenge** since 2013,[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-b-stone-sponsor-2017-1) is an international event for [solar powered cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_powered_car "Solar powered car"). The course is over 3,022 Kilometers (1,878 miles) through the [Australian outback](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_outback "Australian outback"), from [Darwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory") in the [Northern Territory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") to [Adelaide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide") in [South Australia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia").The event was created in 1987 to encourage the development of [solar-powered vehicles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar-powered_vehicle "Solar-powered vehicle")[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-history-2) and has been held fifteen times over its 32-year history. The World Solar Challenge is usually held every two years, but the 2021 event[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-'21-cancel-3) was canceled because of the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), causing a four-year gap between the 2019 and 2023 events. The event was initially held once every three years, and became biennial from the turn of the century. The WSC attracts teams from [universities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universities "Universities"), [corporations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corporation "Corporation"), and [high schools](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High_school "High school") around the world. Team from [Delft University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delft_University_of_Technology "Delft University of Technology") ( [Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")), known as the [Nuna team and cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuna "Nuna"), have won eight out of twelve races since 2001. Since 2007, the WSC has included multiple vehicle classes. In 2013, a radically new _"Cruiser Class"_ was introduced, promotes the technological development of practical, road-legal, and multi-seater solar vehicles. The speed of competing cars has increased from an average speed of 66.9km/h (41.6mph) in 1987 to 88.5km/h (55.0mph) in 1996, to 100km/h (62mph) by 2005. ## Objective The objective of the challenge is to promote the innovation of [solar-powered cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_vehicle "Solar vehicle"). It is a design competition at its core, and every team/car that successfully crosses the finish line is considered successful. Teams from universities and enterprises participate. In 2015, 43 teams from 23 countries competed in the challenge.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-team-2015-4) Except [Nuna team and cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuna "Nuna") from [Delft University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delft_University_of_Technology "Delft University of Technology") ( [Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")), team from [Delft University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delft_University_of_Technology "Delft University of Technology") ( [Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")), known as the [Nuna team and cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuna "Nuna"), have won eight out of twelve races since 2001. The [Tokai Challenger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokai_Challenger "Tokai Challenger"), built by the [Tokai University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokai_University "Tokai University") of Japan, won in 2009 and 2011. The Belgian [Innoptus Solar Team](https://www.solarteam.be/) formerly known as the Agoria Solar Team from [KU Leuven](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/KU_Leuven "KU Leuven") University won in 2019 and 2023. Remarkable technological progress has been achieved since the [General Motors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") led, highly experimental, single-seat _[Sunraycer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunraycer "Sunraycer")_ prototype first won the WSC with an average speed of 66.9km/h (41.6mph). Once competing cars became steadily more capable to match or exceed legal maximum speeds on the Australian highway, the challenge rules were consistently made more demanding and challenging — for instance after [Honda](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honda "Honda")'s _Dream_ car first won with an average speed exceeding 88.5km/h (55.0mph) in 1996. In 2005 the Dutch Nuna team were the first to beat an average speed of 100km/h (62mph). The 2017 Cruiser class winner, the five-seat _Stella Vie_ vehicle, was able to carry an average of 3.4 occupants at an average speed of 69km/h (43mph). Like its two predecessors, the vehicle was successfully road registered by the Dutch team, further emphasizing the great progress in real-world compliance and practicality that has been achieved.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-team-eind-legal-2017-5)[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-autoweek-ketenken-6) ## Challenge strategy | | | | --- | --- | | [![icon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/120px-Question_book-new.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special:EditPage/World_Solar_Challenge "Special:EditPage/World Solar Challenge") by [adding citations to reliable sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability")._(February 2021)__([Learn how and when to remove this message](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))_ | Efficient balancing of power resources and power consumption is the key to success during the challenge. At any moment in time, the optimal driving speed depends on the weather forecast and the remaining capacity of the batteries. The team members in the escort cars will continuously remotely retrieve data from the [solar car](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car "Solar car") about its condition and use these data as input for prior developed computer programs to work out the best driving strategy. It is equally important to charge the batteries as much as possible in periods of daylight when the car is not driving. To capture as much solar energy as possible, the solar panels are generally directed such that these are perpendicular to the incident sun rays. Sometimes the whole solar array is tilted for this purpose. ## Important rules - The timed portion of the challenge stops at the outskirts of Adelaide, 2998km from Darwin. However, for the timings recorded at that point to count, competitors must reach the official finish line in the centre of the city under solar power alone. - As the challenge utilises public roads, the cars have to adhere to the normal traffic regulations. - A minimum of 2 and maximum 4 drivers have to be registered. If the weight of a driver (including clothes) is less than 80kg (180lb), ballast will be added to make up the difference. - Driving time is between 8:00 and 17:00 (from 8a.m. to 5p.m.). In order to select a suitable place for the overnight stop (alongside the highway) it is possible to extend the driving period for a maximum of 10minutes, which extra driving time will be compensated by a starting time delay the next day. - At various points along the route there are checkpoints where every car has to pause for 30minutes. Only limited maintenance tasks (no repairs) are allowed during these compulsory stops. - The capacity of the batteries is limited to a mass for each chemistry (such as Lithium Ion) equivalent to approximately 5kWh maximum. At the start of the route, the batteries may be fully charged. Batteries may not be replaced during the competition, except in the situation of a breakdown. However, in that case, a penalty time will apply. - Except for the maximum outer dimensions, there are no further restrictions on the design and construction of the car. - The deceleration of the dual braking system must be at least 3.8m/s2 (149.6 in/s2). ### Rule evolution - By 2005, several teams were handicapped by the South Australian speed limit of 110km/h (68mph), as well as the difficulties of support crews keeping up with 130km/h (81mph) solar vehicles. It was generally agreed that the challenge of building a solar vehicle capable of crossing Australia at vehicular speeds had been met and exceeded. A new challenge was set: to build a new generation of solar car, which, with little modification, could be the basis for a practical proposition for sustainable transport. - Entrants to the 2007 event chose between racing in the Adventure and Challenge classes. Challenge class cars were restricted to 6square meters of [Si](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") solar collectors (a 25% reduction), and later to 3square meters for [GaAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GaAs "GaAs"), driver access and egress were required to be unaided, seating position upright, steering controlled with a steering wheel, and many new safety requirements were added. Competitors also had to adhere to the new 130km/h (81mph) speed limit across the [Northern Territory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Northern_Territory "Northern Territory") portion of the [Stuart Highway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stuart_Highway "Stuart Highway"). The 2007 event again featured a range of supplementary classes, including the Greenfleet class, which features a range of non-solar energy-efficient vehicles exhibiting their fuel efficiency. - For the 2009 challenge class several new rules were adopted, including the use of profiled tyres. Battery weight limits depend on secondary cell chemistries so that competitors have similar energy storage capabilities. Battery mass is now 20kg for [Li-ion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Li-ion_battery "Li-ion battery") and [Li-polymer battery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lithium_polymer_battery "Lithium polymer battery") (was reduced from 25 and 21kg in the past). - In 2013, a new Cruiser Class was introduced. After the German team of [Bochum University of Applied Sciences](https://www.hochschule-bochum.de/en/) competed with a four-wheeled, multi-seat car, the [_BoCruiser_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PowerCore_SunCruiser#Race "PowerCore SunCruiser") (in 2009), in 2013 a radically new _"Cruiser Class"_ was introduced, stimulating the technological development of practically usable, and ideally road-legal, multi-seater solar vehicles. The route took place in four stages. Final placings were based on a combination of time taken (56.6%), number of passengers carried (5.7%), battery energy from the grid between stages (18.9%), and a subjective assessment of practicality (18.9%). Since its inception, Solar Team Eindhoven's four- and five-seat [_Stella_ solar cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stella_(solar_vehicles) "Stella (solar vehicles)") from [Eindhoven University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eindhoven_University_of_Technology "Eindhoven University of Technology") (Netherlands) won the Cruiser Class in all four events so far. - In the 2015 Cruiser Class regulations, the scoring formula emphasized practicality less than before. Elapsed time will account for 70% of the score, passengers 5%, grid energy use 15%, and practicality 10%. - In 2017, solar array areas were reduced, and the Cruiser Class was changed to a Regularity Trial, with scoring based on energy efficiency and practicality. - In 2025, to slow down the race and show that solar cars are feasible even during winter, the race was shifted by two months to the end of August. To add more emphasis on the _solar_ aspect of the challenge, the solar collector area was also increased to 6m2 while limiting the battery pack capacity to 11 MJ. ## History The idea for the competition originates from Danish-born adventurer [Hans Tholstrup](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hans_Tholstrup "Hans Tholstrup").[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-7)[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-8) He was the first to circumnavigate the Australian continent in a 16-foot (4.9m) open boat. At a later stage in his life he became involved in various competitions with fuel-saving cars and trucks. Already in the 1980s, he became aware of the necessity to explore sustainable energy as a replacement for the limited available fossil fuel. Sponsored by BP, he designed the world's first solar car, called [The Quiet Achiever](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Quiet_Achiever "The Quiet Achiever"), and traversed the 4,052km (2,518mi) between [Sydney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [New South Wales](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") and [Perth](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Perth "Perth"), [Western Australia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") in 20days. That was the precursor of the WSC. After the 4th event, he sold the rights to the state of South Australia and leadership of the event was assumed by Chris Selwood. The event was held every three years until 1999 when it was switched to every two years. ### 1987 Main article: [World Solar Challenge 1987](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge_1987 "World Solar Challenge 1987") The first edition of the World Solar Challenge was run in 1987 when the winning entry, [GM](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Motors_Corporation "General Motors Corporation")'s [Sunraycer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunraycer "Sunraycer") won with an average speed of 67km/h (42mph).[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-9) [Ford Australia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ford_Australia "Ford Australia")'s "Sunchaser" came in second. [South Korea](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") | 73 | ### 2013 Main article: [World Solar Challenge 2013](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge_2013 "World Solar Challenge 2013") The 2013 WSC featured the introduction of the Cruiser Class, which comprised more 'practical' solar cars with 2–4 occupants. The inaugural winner was Solar Team Eindhoven's _[Stella](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stella_(solar_vehicles) "Stella (solar vehicles)")_ from [Eindhoven University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eindhoven_University_of_Technology "Eindhoven University of Technology") in the [Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") with an average speed of 74.52km/h (46.30mph), while second place was taken by the [PowerCore SunCruiser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PowerCore_SunCruiser "PowerCore SunCruiser") vehicle from team [Hochschule Bochum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hochschule_Bochum?action=edit&redlink=1 "Hochschule Bochum") in Germany, who inspired the creation of the Cruiser Class\[ _[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_\] by racing more practical solar cars in previous WSC events. The Australian team, the [University of New South Wales](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_New_South_Wales "University of New South Wales") solar racing team [Sunswift](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunswift "Sunswift") was the fastest competitor to complete the route, but was awarded third place overall after points were awarded for 'practicality' and for [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Stella_Lux%2C_a_solar-powered_family_car_%28cropped%29.jpg/500px-Stella_Lux%2C_a_solar-powered_family_car_%28cropped%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Stella_Lux,_a_solar-powered_family_car_(cropped).jpg) In 2015 [Solar team Eindhoven](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stella_(solar_vehicles) "Stella (solar vehicles)") won the Cruiser Class again with their Stella Lux " [family car](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Family_car "Family car")". The 2015 WSC was held on 15–25 October with the same classes as the 2013 challenge. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/World_Solar_Challenge_2015-Parade_at_Victoria_Sqare_in_Adelaide%2C_Australia.JPG/500px-World_Solar_Challenge_2015-Parade_at_Victoria_Sqare_in_Adelaide%2C_Australia.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:World_Solar_Challenge_2015-Parade_at_Victoria_Sqare_in_Adelaide,_Australia.JPG) World Solar Challenge 2015-Parade at Victoria Square in Adelaide, Australia In the Cruiser Class, the winner was once again Solar Team Eindhoven's _[Stella Lux](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stella_(solar_vehicles) "Stella (solar vehicles)")_ from [Eindhoven University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eindhoven_University_of_Technology "Eindhoven University of Technology") in the [Netherlands](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") with an average speed of 76.73km/h (47.68mph), while the second place team was [Kogakuin University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kogakuin_University "Kogakuin University") from Japan who was the first to cross the finish line, but did not receive as many points for passenger-kilometers and practicality. [Bochum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bochum "Bochum") took 3rd place this year with the latest in their series of cruiser ISTANBUL | Turkey | 10/14/2018 12:16 | | RVCE Solar Car Team | ARKA | India | 10/16/2018 17:00 | | Lodz Solar Team | Eagle Two | Poland | 10/13/2018 15:05 | | TAFE SA | SAV | Australia | 10/13/2018 10:51 | | National Kaohsiung University of Applied Sciences (KUAS) & St. John's University Solar Car Team | Apollo VIII | Taiwan | 10/14/2018 08:46 | ### 2019 The 2019 WSC was held from 13 to 20 October. 53 teams from 24 countries entered the competition,[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-29)[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-abc-2019-fire-vattenvall-30) featuring the same three classes, Challenger (30 teams), Cruiser (23 teams) and Adventure.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-31) In the Challenger class, Agoria Solar Team (formerly Punch Powertrain) won for the first time. Tokai University Solar Car Team finished in second place.[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-2019-agoria-win-32) In the Cruiser class, Solar Team Eindhoven won their fourth consecutive title.[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-team-eindhoven-33) Despite multiple incidents on the road, Team Sonnenwagen Aachen managed to beat other teams and finished in 6th position. Several teams had mishaps. Vattenfall was leading when their car _Nuna X_ caught fire. The driver was uninjured, but the vehicle was destroyed. It was the first no-finish for that team in 20 years.[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-abc-2019-fire-vattenvall-30) Others were badly affected by strong winds. Dutch team Twente was leading the journey at 100km/h (62mph), when their car was forced off the road by winds and rolled over. The driver was taken to hospital. Within 30 minutes team Sonnenwagen Aachen was also blown off the road north of [Coober Pedy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coober_Pedy "Coober Pedy"), the driver was not hurt. An 80km/h (50mph) speed limit was then imposed by event officials, lifted when conditions improved. The day before, wind damage to solar panels put the team from Western Sydney University out of the challenge. The driver of _Agoria_ from Belgium escaped injury when their vehicle was "uprooted" at 100 km/h (62 mph) by severe winds, but still went on to win the Challenger class.[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-abc-2019-fire-vattenvall-30)[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-abc-2019-windy-34) | | Adventure results | | --- | ### 2021 In response to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Australia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Australia "COVID-19 pandemic in Australia") the WSC closed entries three months earlier than normal, on 18 December 2020. They were then to "… review all current government measures relating to social distancing, density and contact tracing, international travel restrictions and isolation requirements."[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-covid-response-35) On 12 February 2021, the South Australian Government confirmed the cancellation of the 2021 staging of the event. While the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") was not explicitly cited as the reason, the "complexities of international border closures" affecting Australia at the time appear to be the primary reason for the event's cancellation. The same statement also noted the next event would take place in October 2023 - at least 962 days from the date of announcement, and resulting in a four-year gap between events.[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-'21-cancel-3) Registered teams should receive a full refund of all fees.[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-wsc-covid-response-35) ### 2023 The 2023 World Solar Challenge was held from October 22-29. At the beginning of the race, 31 teams were participating, with 23 in the Challenger division and 8 in the Cruiser division.[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-36) The Challenger division was won by defending champions Innoptus (formerly Agoria) with an average speed of 88.2km/h, and the Cruiser division was won by [UNSW Sunswift](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNSW_Sunswift "UNSW Sunswift") with a score of 91.1.[\[37\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-37)[\[38\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-38) Uniquely, no Cruisers were able to finish the race this year. Many of the leading teams faced trouble during the competition. Dutch team Top Dutch raced on a perovskite-tandem solar array damaged from testing in the month leading up to race. [Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car_Team "University of Michigan Solar Car Team") experienced electrical issues during qualifying and had to start last. German team Sonnenwagen was blown off the road just outside of [Port Agusta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Port_Augusta "Port Augusta") and had to withdraw due to new regulations.[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-39) Tokai had to stop for several hours on Day 4 to repair their car after sustaining damage from crossing a cattle grid. Kogakuin had consistent problems with their [MPPT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maximum_power_point_tracking "Maximum power point tracking") charge controller, and reported in an Instagram post that their panels were generating less than half the power than they should have been.[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-40) On the fifth day of the competition, only 4 teams (Innoptus, Twente, Brunel, and Michigan) had finished the course, and by the official end of timing, only 12 teams made it to the finish line successfully. | Challenger results | | --- | | 14.7 | | ### 2025 The 2025 World Solar Challenge was held from August 24-31,[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-41) with an entry of 34 teams.[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-42) The new regulations have allowed for an increase in solar array size from 4m2 to 6m2. Also the race was the first held in the Australian winter, creating cooler conditions for drivers but reduced sunlight, increasing the challenge. The Brunel Solar Team from the Netherlands won, narrowly beating fellow Dutch team Twente and Belgium’s Innoptus. Brunel’s katamaran Nuna 13 featured two side fins to capture wind for extra speed and stability.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge#cite_note-43) Notably, the field saw four different teams with fin-like aerodynamic thrust devices on their cars. The concept, while being explored previously, has gained major interest from teams after Innoptus Solar Team’s 2023 win with such a device on their car. | Challenger results | | --- | | **Rank** | Formula Sun Grand Prix

The **Formula Sun Grand Prix** (FSGP) is an annual [solar-car race](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car_race "Solar car race") that takes place on closed-loop race tracks. In the race, teams from colleges and universities throughout North America design, build, test, and race [solar-powered vehicles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_vehicle "Solar vehicle").

Every two years the race serves as a qualifier for the [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") road race.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-1)

## Format and organization

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Format and organization")\]

### Rules

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rules")\]

- The race consists of three days of freely driving laps around the race track.
- The team with the most total laps driven wins.
- The total area of all solar cells and related reflectors, etc., must not exceed 4 square meters (43 sq ft).[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-2)
- The solar array may be reoriented toward the sun for charging batteries for specified time periods before and after race hours.
- Strict specifications and engineering scrutiny process is provided for vehicle configuration, safety requirements, and other standards.
- Previous races have divided teams into "open" and "stock" classes based on levels of solar cell and battery technologies.

## History

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")\]

**Formula Sun Grand Prix** is governed by the [Innovators Educational Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Innovators_Educational_Foundation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Innovators Educational Foundation (page does not exist)"),[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-3) and was started in summer 2000 at [Heartland Park](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heartland_Park_Topeka "Heartland Park Topeka") race track in [Topeka, Kansas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topeka,_Kansas "Topeka, Kansas"), in conjunction with a [solar-bike](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_bike&action=edit&redlink=1 "Solar bike (page does not exist)") race.

It has served as a qualifying race for the biennial [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") road race.

The race was held there every year through 2005 until the 2007 American Solar Challenge was canceled due to funding issues. It resumed in 2009 and has been held every year since then at a few different venues.

### 2000

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2000")\]

The inaugural race was held at [Heartland Park Topeka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heartland_Park_Topeka "Heartland Park Topeka") race track in [Topeka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topeka "Topeka"), [Kansas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"). It was won by [Rose-Hulman Institute of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose-Hulman_Institute_of_Technology "Rose-Hulman Institute of Technology")'s _Solar Phantom V_.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-4) The stock class was won by the [University of Missouri-Rolla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Missouri_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Missouri University of Science and Technology")'s _Solar Miner II_.

### 2001

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2001")\]

**FSGP 2001** served as a qualifier for the 2001 [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") and was the only year when multiple events were held.

- The first event was again held at [Heartland Park Topeka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heartland_Park_Topeka "Heartland Park Topeka") and the [Rose-Hulman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rose-Hulman_Institute_of_Technology "Rose-Hulman Institute of Technology") again took first place overall[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-5) with _Solar Phantom VI_, while the stock class was won by the co-op team from [Minnesota State University, Mankato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minnesota_State_University,_Mankato "Minnesota State University, Mankato") and [Winona State University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winona_State_University "Winona State University").

### 2003

### 2015

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2015")\]

**FSGP 2015** was held again at [Circuit of the Americas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circuit_of_the_Americas "Circuit of the Americas") on July 29–31, 2015. It was won by [Iowa State University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iowa_State_University "Iowa State University") with 223 total laps and a fast lap of 4:30.444, both track records for solar cars.[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-16) Hot weather forced most of the teams to have to pull into the pits at times to cool their batteries. The [University of Calgary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Calgary "University of Calgary") became the first Cruiser team to compete in FSGP.

### 2018

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2018")\]

**FSGP 2018** was held at Motorsports Park Hastings in [Hastings, Nebraska](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hastings,_Nebraska "Hastings, Nebraska") on July 10–12, 2018. This year featured the first ever Multi-Occupant Vehicle (MOV) class at FSGP, which was won by [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project") with triple the score of the runner-up, setting a record as the first team to win FSGP 5 times. The Single-Occupant Vehicle (SOV) class was won by [Polytechnique Montreal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytechnique_Montreal "Polytechnique Montreal") with 403 laps.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-19)

### 2019

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2019")\]

**FSGP 2019** was held at [Circuit of the Americas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circuit_of_the_Americas "Circuit of the Americas") in [Austin, Texas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas") on July 4–6, 2019. The Multi-Occupant Vehicle (MOV) class was won by [University of Calgary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Calgary "University of Calgary") with a Score of 24.188. The Single-Occupant Vehicle (SOV) class was won by [Polytechnique Montreal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytechnique_Montreal "Polytechnique Montreal") with 230 laps.[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-20)

### 2020

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: 2020")\]

**FSGP 2020** was planned to return to [Heartland Park Topeka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heartland_Park_Topeka "Heartland Park Topeka") on July 10-16, 2020,[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-21) but was postponed to September[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-22) and ultimately cancelled[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-23) due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").

### 2021

The Multi-Occupant Vehicle (MOV) class was won by [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota") with a Score of 26.873.
