### 2023

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: 2023")\]

Electrek FSGP 2023 was held at Heartland Motorsports Park. Topeka, KS June 27 - July 2. The Single-Occupant Vehicle (SOV) class was won by the University of Florida - 707.5 miles (283 laps). The Multi-Occupant Vehicle (MOV) class was won by [Polytechnique Montréal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytechnique_Montr%C3%A9al "Polytechnique Montréal") with a 137.3 points / 1,315 person miles (263 laps).

### 2024

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: 2024")\]

[![Three solar cars enter the downhill to the "sinkhole" feature at the National Corvette Museum Motorsports Park during Electrek FSGP 2024. Leading is car #828 from Appalachian State - a two seater car in the MOV Class. Behind them are two cars in the SOV class: #5 from the University of Florida and #6 from Berkeley.](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Solar_Car_on_the_Track_at_FSGP_2024.jpg/250px-Solar_Car_on_the_Track_at_FSGP_2024.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_Car_on_the_Track_at_FSGP_2024.jpg) Electrek FSGP 2024. Left to Right: #828 [Appalachian State University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Appalachian_State_University "Appalachian State University"); #5 [University of Florida](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Florida "University of Florida"); #6 [University of California, Berkeley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley")

Electrek FSGP 2024 was held at the [National Corvette Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Corvette_Museum "National Corvette Museum") Motorsports Park in Bowling Green, KY from July 16-18th. The 2024 event served as the qualifier for the Electrek [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") 2024 which immediately followed FSGP. Difficult weather resulted in many teams not meeting the qualification requirements for ASC with some being granted provisional qualification. The Single Occupant Class was won by team Éclipse from [École de technologie supérieure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89cole_de_technologie_sup%C3%A9rieure "École de technologie supérieure") with 215 lap completed for a total of 677.25 miles. The Multi-Occupant Class was also won by a team from [Montreal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") with Project Esteban from [Polytechnique Montréal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytechnique_Montr%C3%A9al "Polytechnique Montréal") earning the top score of 88.57.[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-27)

### 2025

\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: 2025")\]

For the second year in a row, Electrek FSGP 2025 was held at the [National Corvette Museum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Corvette_Museum "National Corvette Museum") Motorsports Park in [Bowling Green](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bowling_Green,_Kentucky "Bowling Green, Kentucky"), KY. It took place between July 3rd-6th, with 33 teams competing in this year's event.[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-28) The Single Occupant Class was won by team Illini Solar Car from the [University of Illinois](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign"), with their car Calypso completing 223 laps for a total of 702.5 miles. For the fourth consecutive year, the Multi-Occupant Class was won by a team Esteban from [Polytechnique Montréal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytechnique_Montr%C3%A9al "Polytechnique Montréal"), with a score of 148.4.[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Formula_Sun_Grand_Prix#cite_note-29)

Electric car powered by solar energy

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Solar_Car_Tokai_Challenger.JPG/960px-Solar_Car_Tokai_Challenger.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_Car_Tokai_Challenger.JPG)

[Tokai Challenger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokai_Challenger "Tokai Challenger"), the winner of the 2009 [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge"), with an average speed of 100.5 km/h (62 mph) over the 2,998 km (1,858 mi.) race

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Lightyear_one_Fully_Charged_Europe_2022_6.jpg/960px-Lightyear_one_Fully_Charged_Europe_2022_6.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lightyear_one_Fully_Charged_Europe_2022_6.jpg)

The [Lightyear One](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lightyear_0 "Lightyear 0"), a solar-electric passenger car went into limited production in December 2022. Production stopped a month later.

A **solar car** is a [solar vehicle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_vehicle "Solar vehicle") for use on public roads or race tracks. Solar vehicles are [electric vehicles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_vehicles "Electric vehicles") that use self-contained [solar cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cells "Solar cells") to provide full or partial power to the vehicle via sunlight. Solar vehicles typically contain a [rechargeable battery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rechargeable_battery "Rechargeable battery") to help regulate and store the energy from the solar cells and from [regenerative braking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regenerative_braking "Regenerative braking"). Some solar cars can be plugged into external power sources to supplement the power of sunlight used to charge their battery.

Solar cars combine technology typically used in the [aerospace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace"), [bicycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bicycle "Bicycle"), [alternative energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alternative_energy "Alternative energy") and [automotive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automotive "Automotive") industries. The design of solar vehicles always emphasizes [energy efficiency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use") to make maximum use of the limited amount of energy they can receive from sunlight. Most solar cars have been built for the purpose of [solar car races](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car_racing "Solar car racing"). However several prototypes of solar cars designed for use on public roads have been designed and built.

There are various solar car competitions around the world that are generally partaken by collegiate and company teams. The most notable competitions is the [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge"), which is an international competition that takes place in Australia. Some other competitions include the [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") and the United Solar Challenge.

As of December 2022, only one solar car had reached production, the [Lightyear 0](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lightyear_0 "Lightyear 0"), but at the limited delivery rate of one car a week.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-1) In January 2023 the company halted vehicle manufacture and sales, and the vehicle maker's production company, Atlas Technologies B.V., requested bankruptcy protection.[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-2)[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-3)

The [Aptera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aptera_(solar_electric_vehicle) "Aptera (solar electric vehicle)")'s production is expected to start in 2025.[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-4)

Solar cars depend on a [solar array](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_array "Solar array") that uses [photovoltaic cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaic_cell "Photovoltaic cell") (PV cells) to convert sunlight into electricity. Unlike solar thermal energy which converts solar energy to heat, PV cells directly convert sunlight into electricity.[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-5) When sunlight (photons) strike PV cells, they excite electrons and allow them to flow, creating an electric current. PV cells are made of semiconductor materials such as [silicon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") and alloys of indium, gallium and nitrogen. [Crystalline silicon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crystalline_silicon "Crystalline silicon") is the most common material used and has an efficiency rate of 15–25%.

## History

The first model solar car was the [Sunmobile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunmobile?action=edit&redlink=1 "Sunmobile"), a tiny 15-inch vehicle created by General Motors employee William G. Cobb. He displayed it in 1955 at the Chicago Powerama convention. It was made up of 12 selenium photovoltaic cells and a small electric motor.[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-6)

In 1962 the International Rectifier Company added a solar panel to a 1912 Baker Electric, and unveiled the first solar car that could carry passengers.[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-7)

## Solar array

The solar array consists of hundreds of [solar cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cells "Solar cells") converting sunlight into electricity. In order to construct an array, PV cells are placed together to form modules which are placed together to form an array.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-8) The larger arrays in use can produce over 2 kilowatts (2.6hp).

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Pv_cells.svg/960px-Pv_cells.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Pv_cells.svg) [Cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell"), [modules](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_module "Solar module"), and [arrays](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_array "Solar array")

The solar array can be mounted in six ways:

- **horizontal**. This most common arrangement gives most overall power during most of the day in low latitudes or higher latitude summers and offers little interaction with the wind. Horizontal arrays can be integrated or be in the form of a free canopy.
- **vertical**. This arrangement is sometimes found in free standing or integrated [sails](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sails "Sails") to harness wind energy.[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-Wind-9) Useful solar power is limited to mornings, evenings, or winters and when the vehicle is pointing in the right direction.
- **adjustable**. Free solar arrays can often be tilted around the axis of travel in order to increase power when the sun is low and well to the side. An alternative is to tilt the whole vehicle when parked. Two-axis adjustment is only found on marine vehicles, where the aerodynamic resistance is of less importance than with road vehicles.
- [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Squad_Solar_Car_%28Fully_Charged_2022%29.jpg/500px-Squad_Solar_Car_%28Fully_Charged_2022%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Squad_Solar_Car_(Fully_Charged_2022).jpg) The [Squad Solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Squad_Solar "Squad Solar") City Car, as presented of the Fully Charged 2022 event in Amsterdam. **integrated**. Some vehicles cover every available surface with solar cells. Some of the cells will be at an optimal angle whereas others will be shaded.
- **trailer**. Solar trailers are especially useful for retrofitting existing vehicles with little stability, e.g. bicycles. Some trailers also include the batteries and others also the drive motor.
- **remote**. By mounting the solar array at a stationary location instead of the vehicle, power can be maximised and resistance minimized. The virtual grid-connection however involves more electrical losses than with true solar vehicles and the battery must be larger.

The choice of solar array geometry involves an optimization between power output, aerodynamic resistance and vehicle mass, as well as practical considerations. For example, a free horizontal canopy gives 2-3 times the surface area of a vehicle with integrated cells but offers better cooling of the cells and shading of the riders. There are also thin flexible solar arrays in development.

Solar arrays on solar cars are mounted and encapsulated very differently from stationary solar arrays. Solar arrays on solar cars are usually mounted using industrial grade double-sided adhesive tape right onto the car's body. The arrays are encapsulated using thin layers of Tedlar.

Some solar cars use gallium arsenide solar cells, with efficiencies around thirty percent. Other solar cars use silicon solar cells, with efficiencies around twenty percent.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-10)

## Batteries

The battery pack in a typical solar car is sufficient to allow the car to go 250 miles (400km) without sun, and allow the car to continuously travel at speeds of 60mph (97km/h).

## Motors

The motors used in solar cars typically generate about 2 or 3 horsepower,[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-11) yet experimental light solar cars may attain the same speed as a typical family car (100mph (160km/h)).[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-12)

## Races

Main article: [Solar car racing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car_racing "Solar car racing")

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Solar_Vehicles_-_Winnipeg.jpg/500px-Solar_Vehicles_-_Winnipeg.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_Vehicles_-_Winnipeg.jpg) Solar cars from [University of Michigan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Michigan_Solar_Car_Team "University of Michigan Solar Car Team") and [University of Minnesota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Minnesota_Solar_Vehicle_Project "University of Minnesota Solar Vehicle Project") heading west toward the finish line in the 2005 North American Solar Challenge

Two solar car races are the World Solar Challenge and the American Solar Challenge, overland road rally-style competitions contested by a variety of university and corporate teams.

The [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") features a field of competitors from around the world who race to cross the [Australian continent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_continent "Australian continent"), over a distance of 3,000 kilometres (1,900mi). Speeds of the vehicles have steadily increased. For example, the high speeds of 2005 race participants led to the rules being changed for solar cars starting in the 2007 race and 2014 also.

The [American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge"), previously known as the 'North American Solar Challenge' and 'Sunrayce USA', features mostly collegiate teams racing in timed intervals in the United States and Canada. This race also changed rules for the most recent race due to teams reaching the regulated speed limits. The most recent American Solar Challenge took place from [Independence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri"), [Missouri](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") to [Twin Falls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Twin_Falls,_Idaho "Twin Falls, Idaho"), [Idaho](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") from July 9 to July 16, 2022.[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-13)

The [Solar Car Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Car_Challenge "Solar Car Challenge") is an annual solar-powered car race for high school students. The event attracts teams from around the world, but mostly from American high schools. The race was first held in 1995. Each event is the end product of a two-year education cycle launched by the Winston Solar Car Team. In odd-numbered years, the race is a road course that starts at the Dell Diamond in Round Rock, Texas; the end of the course varies from year to year. In even-numbered years, the race is a track race around the Texas Motor Speedway.

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3f/Solar_cells_power_Electric_Car.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_cells_power_Electric_Car.jpg)[Solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar-charged_vehicle "Solar-charged vehicle") cells spread over the top of this car produce enough [energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") to keep its [electric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_power "Electric power") motor running

The [South African Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_African_Solar_Challenge "South African Solar Challenge") is a bi-annual two-week race of solar-powered cars through the length and breadth of South Africa. Teams will have to build their own cars, design their own engineering systems and race those same machines through the most demanding terrain that solar cars have ever seen. The 2008 race proved that this event can attract the interest of the public, and that it has the necessary international backing from the FIA. Late in September, all entrants will take off from Pretoria and make their way to Cape Town via the N1, then drive along the coast to Durban, before climbing the escarpment on their way back to the finish line in Pretoria 10 days later. In 2008 the event was endorsed by International Solarcar Federation (ISF), [Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Internationale_de_l'Automobile "Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile") (FIA), [World Wildlife Fund](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Wildlife_Fund "World Wildlife Fund") (WWF) making it the first Solar Race to receive endorsement from these 3 organizations.

There are other distance races, such as [Suzuka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suzuka_Circuit "Suzuka Circuit"), [Phaethon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phaethon "Phaethon"), [WGC (WSR/JISFC/WSBR)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Green_Challenge?action=edit&redlink=1 "World Green Challenge") and the [World Solar Rally in Taiwan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2006_World_Solar_Rally_in_Taiwan "2006 World Solar Rally in Taiwan"). Suzuka and WGC is a yearly track race in [Japan](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Japan "Japan") and Phaethon was part of the Cultural Olympiad in [Greece](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greece "Greece") right before the [2004 Olympics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2004_Olympics "2004 Olympics").

## Speed record

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Solar_car_LSR.jpg/500px-Solar_car_LSR.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_car_LSR.jpg) Sunswift IV and control vehicle during speed record attempts at HMAS _Albatross_

[Guinness World Records](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records") recognize a land speed record for vehicles powered only by solar panels. This record is currently held by the Sky Ace TIGA from the [Ashiya University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ashiya_University "Ashiya University").[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-14) The record of 91.332km/h (56.75mph) was set on 20 August 2014 at the [Shimojishima Airport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shimojishima_Airport "Shimojishima Airport"), in [Miyakojima](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miyakojima "Miyakojima"), [Okinawa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture"), Japan. The previous record was held by the [University of New South Wales](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_New_South_Wales "University of New South Wales") with the car [Sunswift IV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunswift "Sunswift"). Its 25-kilogram (55lb) battery was removed so the vehicle was powered only by its solar panels.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-15) The record of 88.8km/h (55.2mph) was set on 7 January 2011 at the naval air base [HMAS _Albatross_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HMAS_Albatross_(air_station) "HMAS Albatross (air station)") in [Nowra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nowra "Nowra"), breaking the record previously held by the [General Motors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") car [Sunraycer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunraycer "Sunraycer") of 78.3 kilometres per hour (48.7mph). The record takes place over a flying 500 metres (1,600ft) stretch, and is the average of two runs in opposite directions.

## Cars for public use

See also: [List of prototype solar-powered cars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_prototype_solar-powered_cars "List of prototype solar-powered cars")

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Volkswagen_ID._Buzz_solar_roof.webp/500px-Volkswagen_ID._Buzz_solar_roof.webp.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Volkswagen_ID._Buzz_solar_roof.webp) 3D design of the [Volkswagen ID. Buzz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volkswagen_ID._Buzz "Volkswagen ID. Buzz") with a solar roof[\[16\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-16)[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Sono_Sion_2017-08-18_1213_%28cropped%29.JPG/500px-Sono_Sion_2017-08-18_1213_%28cropped%29.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sono_Sion_2017-08-18_1213_(cropped).JPG) Prototype of the [Sion family car](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sion_(electric_car) "Sion (electric car)")

The first solar family car was built in 2013.[\[17\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-17) Researchers at [Case Western Reserve University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Case_Western_Reserve_University "Case Western Reserve University"), have also developed a solar car which can recharge more quickly, due to materials used in the solar panels.[\[18\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-18)

Chinese solar panel manufacturer [Hanergy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hanergy "Hanergy") plans to build and sell solar cars equipped with [lithium-ion batteries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lithium-ion_batteries "Lithium-ion batteries") to consumers in China.[\[19\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-19)[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-20) Hanergy says that five to six hours of sunlight should allow the cars' thin-film solar cells to generate 8–10kWh of energy a day, allowing the car to travel about 80km (50mi) on solar power alone.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-21) Maximum range is about 350km (217mi).

In June 2019 the solar-electric [Lightyear One](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lightyear_One "Lightyear One") was announced, since renamed the [Lightyear 0](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lightyear_0 "Lightyear 0"). Designed by former engineers from [Tesla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tesla_Inc. "Tesla Inc.") and [Ferrari](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ferrari "Ferrari"), the car's hood and roof are composed of solar panels. The vehicle also charges on regular electric power as well as fast-charging stations.[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-22) In September 2021, the company Lightyear was reported to have raised enough money to bring the vehicle to limited production, at a cost of €149,000, delivering the first units in 2022.[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-23)

[![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Aptera_Side_View_with_Open_Door.jpg/500px-Aptera_Side_View_with_Open_Door.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Aptera_Side_View_with_Open_Door.jpg) First prototype of the Aptera solar powered EV

In August 2019, [Aptera Motors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aptera_Motors "Aptera Motors") announced a funding campaign for a solar-powered, very efficient "Never Charge" EV, the [Aptera](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aptera_(solar_electric_vehicle) "Aptera (solar electric vehicle)"), with up to a 1,600km (1,000mi) range.[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-24)[\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-25) That funding campaign was successful and the first Aptera prototype was shown and the EV launched on December 4, 2020.[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-26) As of July 2023, three generations of prototypes have been built and shown, production equipment and tooling is being acquired and fundraising continues.

In July 2020, the German car manufacturer [Audi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Audi "Audi") signed an MOU with an Israeli start-up [Apollo-Power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apollo-Power?action=edit&redlink=1 "Apollo-Power") for development plan to incorporate the company proprietary lightweight flexible panels into Car parts.
Apollo-Power's Agenda is to turn every car in the world to become Solar.

In 2019 the Squad Solar City Car by Squad Mobility was announced and in May 2022 it was finally presented in the Media. The Squad Solar City Car is the first Solar Micro Car for (sub)urban use.

In May 2025, Solar Transport Systems launched the INTI Solar Neighborhood Car, designed as a solar electric vehicle for local transportation. It offers both solar-powered range and solar battery charged driving capabilities.[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-BrevardBusinessNews-27)[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_car#cite_note-FloridaToday-28)

# Solar energy Solar energy Radiant light and heat from the Sun, harnessed with technology This article is about radiant light and heat from the Sun that is harnessed using a range of technologies. For more detail about the generation of electricity using solar energy, see [Solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power"). For the academic journal, see [Solar Energy (journal)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Energy_(journal) "Solar Energy (journal)"). | | | | --- | --- | | [![icon](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Duplicate_content.svg/120px-Duplicate_content.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Duplicate_content.svg) | This article **[duplicates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:CFORK "Wikipedia:CFORK") the scope of other articles**, specifically [Solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power"). Please [discuss this issue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Solar_energy "Talk:Solar energy") and help introduce a [summary style](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Summary_style "Wikipedia:Summary style") to the article._(November 2022)_ | [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Sunset_over_the_gulf_of_Mexico_-_iss042e034066.jpg/960px-Sunset_over_the_gulf_of_Mexico_-_iss042e034066.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sunset_over_the_gulf_of_Mexico_-_iss042e034066.jpg) The Sun produces [electromagnetic radiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") that can be harnessed as useful energy. **Solar energy** is the [radiant energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiant_energy "Radiant energy") from the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun "Sun")'s [light](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") and [heat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat "Heat"), which can be harnessed using a range of [technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technologies "Technologies") such as [solar electricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_electricity "Solar electricity"), [solar thermal energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_thermal_energy "Solar thermal energy") (including [solar water heating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_water_heating "Solar water heating")) and [solar architecture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_architecture "Solar architecture").[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ie11-1)[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-2)[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-3) It is an essential source of [renewable energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"), and its technologies are broadly characterized as either [passive solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_solar "Passive solar") or active solar depending on how they capture and distribute solar energy or convert it into solar power. Active solar techniques include the use of [photovoltaic systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaic_system "Photovoltaic system"), [concentrated solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power "Concentrated solar power"), and solar water heating to harness the energy. Passive solar techniques include designing a building for better [daylighting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daylighting_(architecture) "Daylighting (architecture)"), selecting materials with favorable [thermal mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_mass "Thermal mass") or light-dispersing properties, and organizing spaces that [naturally circulate air](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ventilation_(architecture) "Ventilation (architecture)"). In 2011, the [International Energy Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") said that "the development of affordable, inexhaustible and clean solar energy technologies will have huge longer-term benefits. It will increase countries' [energy security](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_security "Energy security") through reliance on an indigenous, inexhaustible, and mostly import-independent resource, enhance [sustainability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability"), reduce [pollution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pollution "Pollution"), lower the costs of [mitigating global warming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") .... these advantages are global".[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ie11-1)[\[4\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-rev-4) ## Potential Further information: [Solar radiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Breakdown_of_the_incoming_solar_energy.svg/500px-Breakdown_of_the_incoming_solar_energy.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Breakdown_of_the_incoming_solar_energy.svg) About half the incoming solar energy reaches the Earth's surface. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Solar_land_area.png/500px-Solar_land_area.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_land_area.png) Average [insolation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insolation "Insolation"). The theoretical area of the small black dots is sufficient to supply the [world's total energy needs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_energy_consumption "World energy consumption") of 18 [TW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terawatt "Terawatt") with solar power. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/World_GHI_Solar-resource-map_GlobalSolarAtlas_World-Bank-Esmap-Solargis.png/960px-World_GHI_Solar-resource-map_GlobalSolarAtlas_World-Bank-Esmap-Solargis.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:World_GHI_Solar-resource-map_GlobalSolarAtlas_World-Bank-Esmap-Solargis.png) Global map of [horizontal irradiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horizontal_irradiation "Horizontal irradiation")[\[5\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-5) The Earth receives 174 [petawatts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Petawatt "Petawatt") (PW) of incoming solar radiation ( [insolation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insolation "Insolation")) at the upper [atmosphere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth's_atmosphere "Earth's atmosphere").[\[6\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-6) Approximately 30% is reflected back to space while the rest, 122 PW, is absorbed by clouds, oceans and land masses. The [spectrum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum") of solar light at the Earth's surface is mostly spread across the [visible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visible_light "Visible light") and [near-infrared](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Near-infrared "Near-infrared") ranges with a small part in the [near-ultraviolet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Near-ultraviolet "Near-ultraviolet").[\[7\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-7) Most of the world's population live in areas with insolation levels of 150–300 watts/m2, or 3.5–7.0 [kWh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilowatt-hour "Kilowatt-hour")/m2 per day.[\[8\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-8) Solar radiation is absorbed by the Earth's land surface, oceans – which cover about 71% of the globe – and atmosphere. Warm air containing evaporated water from the oceans rises, causing [atmospheric circulation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atmospheric_circulation "Atmospheric circulation") or [convection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convection "Convection"). When the air reaches a high altitude, where the temperature is low, water vapor condenses into clouds, which rain onto the Earth's surface, completing the [water cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_cycle "Water cycle"). The [latent heat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latent_heat "Latent heat") of water condensation amplifies convection, producing atmospheric phenomena such as wind, [cyclones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclone "Cyclone") and [anticyclones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anticyclone "Anticyclone").[\[9\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-9) Sunlight absorbed by the oceans and land masses keeps the surface at an average temperature of 14°C.[\[10\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-10) By [photosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis"), green plants convert solar energy into chemically stored energy, which produces food, wood and the [biomass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") from which [fossil fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels") are derived.[\[11\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-11) The total solar energy absorbed by Earth's atmosphere, oceans and land masses is approximately 122 PW·year = 3,850,000 [exajoules](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joule#Multiples "Joule") (EJ) per year.[\[12\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Smil_2006,_p._12-12) In 2002 (2019), this was more energy in one hour (one hour and 25 minutes) than the world used in one year.[\[13\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-13)[\[14\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-14) Photosynthesis captures approximately 3,000EJ per year in biomass.[\[15\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-15) | Solar | 3,850,000 | [Exajoule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orders_of_magnitude_(energy) "Orders of magnitude (energy)") (EJ) = 1018 [J](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joule "Joule") = 278 [TWh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terawatt-hour "Terawatt-hour")
2 Consumption as of year 2019 | Yearly solar fluxes & human consumption1 The potential solar energy that could be used by humans differs from the amount of solar energy present near the surface of the planet because factors such as geography, time variation, cloud cover, and the land available to humans limit the amount of solar energy that we can acquire. In 2021, [Carbon Tracker Initiative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_Tracker_Initiative "Carbon Tracker Initiative") estimated the land area needed to generate all our energy from solar alone was 450,000 [km2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square_kilometre "Square kilometre") — or about the same as the area of [Sweden](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), or the area of [Morocco](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), or the area of [California](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/California "California") (0.3% of the Earth's total land area).[\[20\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-20) Solar technologies are categorized as either passive or active depending on the way they capture, convert and distribute sunlight and enable solar energy to be harnessed at different levels around the world, mostly depending on the distance from the Equator. Although solar energy refers primarily to the use of solar radiation for practical ends, all types of renewable energy, other than [geothermal power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power") and [tidal power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tidal_power "Tidal power"), are derived either directly or indirectly from the Sun. Active solar techniques use photovoltaics, [concentrated solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power "Concentrated solar power"), [solar thermal collectors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_thermal_collector "Solar thermal collector"), pumps, and fans to convert sunlight into useful output. Passive solar techniques include selecting materials with favorable thermal properties, designing spaces that naturally circulate air, and referencing\[ _[clarification needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")_\] the position of a building to the Sun. Active solar technologies increase the supply of energy and are considered [supply side](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supply_side "Supply side") technologies, while passive solar technologies reduce the need for alternative resources and are generally considered demand-side technologies.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-IEA_Solar_Thermal-21) In 2000, the [United Nations Development Programme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme"), UN Department of Economic and Social Affairs, and [World Energy Council](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Energy_Council "World Energy Council") published an estimate of the potential solar energy that could be used by humans each year. This took into account factors such as insolation, cloud cover, and the land that is usable by humans. It was stated that solar energy has a global potential of 1,600 to 49,800 exajoules (4.4×1014 to 1.4×1016kWh) per year _(see table below)_.[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-World_Energy_Assessment-22) | Region | North America | Latin America
