_Source:_ [United Nations Development Programme](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") – World Energy Assessment (2000)[\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-World_Energy_Assessment-22) | Annual solar energy potential by region (Exajoules) [\[22\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-World_Energy_Assessment-22) ## Thermal energy Main article: [Solar thermal energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_thermal_energy "Solar thermal energy") Solar thermal technologies can be used for water heating, space heating, space cooling and process heat generation.[\[23\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-23) ### Early commercial adaptation In 1878, at the Universal Exposition in Paris, [Augustin Mouchot](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augustin_Mouchot "Augustin Mouchot") successfully demonstrated a solar steam engine but could not continue development because of cheap coal and other factors. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/US_Patent_1240890.pdf/page1-500px-US_Patent_1240890.pdf.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:US_Patent_1240890.pdf) 1917 patent drawing of Shuman's solar collector In 1897, [Frank Shuman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank_Shuman "Frank Shuman"), a US inventor, engineer and solar energy pioneer built a small demonstration solar engine that worked by reflecting solar energy onto square boxes filled with ether, which has a lower boiling point than water and were fitted internally with black pipes which in turn powered a steam engine. In 1908 Shuman formed the Sun Power Company with the intent of building larger solar power plants. He, along with his technical advisor A.S.E. Ackermann and British physicist Sir [Charles Vernon Boys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C._V._Boys "C. V. Boys"),[\[24\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Kryza2003-24) developed an improved system using mirrors to reflect solar energy upon collector boxes, increasing heating capacity to the extent that water could now be used instead of ether. Shuman then constructed a full-scale steam engine powered by low-pressure water, enabling him to patent the entire solar engine system by 1912. Shuman built the world's first [solar thermal power station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_thermal_energy "Solar thermal energy") in [Maadi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maadi "Maadi"), [Egypt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), between 1912 and 1913. His plant used [parabolic troughs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parabolic_trough "Parabolic trough") to power a 45–52 kilowatts (60–70 [hp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horsepower "Horsepower")) engine that pumped more than 22,000 litres (4,800impgal; 5,800USgal) of water per minute from the [Nile River](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River") to adjacent cotton fields. Although the outbreak of World War I and the discovery of [cheap oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") in the 1930s discouraged the advancement of solar energy, Shuman's vision, and basic design were resurrected in the 1970s with a new wave of interest in solar thermal energy.[\[25\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-25) In 1916 Shuman was quoted in the media advocating solar energy's utilization, saying: > We have proved the commercial profit of sun power in the tropics and have more particularly proved that after our stores of oil and coal are exhausted the human race can receive unlimited power from the rays of the Sun. —Frank Shuman, New York Times, 2 July 1916[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-nytimes.com-26) ### Water heating Main articles: [Solar hot water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_hot_water "Solar hot water") and [Solar combisystem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_combisystem "Solar combisystem") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Twice_Cropped_Zonnecollectoren.JPG/500px-Twice_Cropped_Zonnecollectoren.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Twice_Cropped_Zonnecollectoren.JPG) Solar water heaters facing the [Sun](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sun "Sun") to maximize gain Solar hot water systems use sunlight to heat water. In middle geographical latitudes (between 40degrees north and 40degrees south), 60 to 70% of the domestic hot water use, with water temperatures up to 60°C (140°F), can be provided by solar heating systems.[\[27\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-27) The most common types of solar water heaters are evacuated tube collectors (44%) and glazed flat plate collectors (34%) generally used for domestic hot water; and unglazed plastic collectors (21%) used mainly to heat swimming pools.[\[28\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-28) As of 2015, the total installed capacity of solar hot water systems was approximately 436 [thermal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GWth "GWth") [gigawatt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gigawatt "Gigawatt") (GWth), and China is the world leader in their deployment with 309GWth installed, taken up 71% of the market.[\[29\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ADB-China2019-29) [Israel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Israel "Israel") and [Cyprus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") are the per capita leaders in the use of solar hot water systems with over 90% of homes using them.[\[30\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-30) In the United States, Canada, and Australia, heating swimming pools is the dominant application of solar hot water with an installed capacity of 18GWth as of 2005.[\[21\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-IEA_Solar_Thermal-21) ### Heating, cooling and ventilation Main articles: [Solar heating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_heating "Solar heating"), [Thermal mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_mass "Thermal mass"), [Solar chimney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_chimney "Solar chimney"), and [Solar air conditioning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_air_conditioning "Solar air conditioning") In the United States, [heating, ventilation and air conditioning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HVAC "HVAC") (HVAC) systems account for 30% (4.65EJ/yr) of the energy used in commercial buildings and nearly 50% (10.1EJ/yr) of the energy used in residential buildings.[\[31\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-31)[\[32\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-32) Solar heating, cooling and ventilation technologies can be used to offset a portion of this energy. Use of solar for heating can roughly be divided into [passive solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_solar_building_design "Passive solar building design") concepts and [active solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_thermal_energy "Solar thermal energy") concepts, depending on whether active elements such as [sun tracking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_tracker "Solar tracker") and solar concentrator optics are used. [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/8a/Flipped_MIT_Solar_One_house.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Flipped_MIT_Solar_One_house.png) [MIT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology")'s Solar House #1, built in 1939 in the US, used [seasonal thermal energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seasonal_thermal_energy_storage "Seasonal thermal energy storage") for year-round heating. Thermal mass is any material that can be used to store heat—heat from the Sun in the case of solar energy. Common thermal mass materials include stone, cement, and water. Historically they have been used in arid climates or warm temperate regions to keep buildings cool by absorbing solar energy during the day and radiating stored heat to the cooler atmosphere at night. However, they can be used in cold temperate areas to maintain warmth as well. The size and placement of thermal mass depend on several factors such as climate, daylighting, and shading conditions. When duly incorporated, thermal mass maintains space temperatures in a comfortable range and reduces the need for auxiliary heating and cooling equipment.[\[33\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-33) A [solar chimney](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_chimney "Solar chimney") (or thermal chimney, in this context) is a passive solar ventilation system composed of a vertical shaft connecting the interior and exterior of a building. As the chimney warms, the air inside is heated, causing an [updraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Updraft "Updraft") that pulls air through the building. Performance can be improved by using glazing and thermal mass materials[\[34\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-34) in a way that mimics greenhouses. [Deciduous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees and plants have been promoted as a means of controlling solar heating and cooling. When planted on the southern side of a building in the northern hemisphere or the northern side in the southern hemisphere, their leaves provide shade during the summer, while the bare limbs allow light to pass during the winter.[\[35\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-35) Since bare, leafless trees shade 1/3 to 1/2 of incident solar radiation, there is a balance between the benefits of summer shading and the corresponding loss of winter heating.[\[36\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-36) In climates with significant heating loads, deciduous trees should not be planted on the Equator-facing side of a building because they will interfere with winter solar availability. They can, however, be used on the east and west sides to provide a degree of summer shading without appreciably affecting winter [solar gain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_gain "Solar gain").[\[37\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-37) ### Cooking Main article: [Solar cooker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cooker "Solar cooker") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Auroville_Solar_Bowl.JPG/500px-Auroville_Solar_Bowl.JPG)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Auroville_Solar_Bowl.JPG) Parabolic dish produces steam for cooking, in [Auroville](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Auroville "Auroville"), India. Solar cookers use sunlight for cooking, drying, and [pasteurization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pasteurization "Pasteurization"). They can be grouped into three broad categories: box cookers, panel cookers, and reflector cookers.[\[38\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-38) The simplest solar cooker is the box cooker first built by [Horace de Saussure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horace_de_Saussure "Horace de Saussure") in 1767.[\[39\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-39) A basic box cooker consists of an insulated container with a transparent lid. It can be used effectively with partially overcast skies and will typically reach temperatures of 90–150°C (194–302°F).[\[40\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-40) Panel cookers use a reflective panel to direct sunlight onto an insulated container and reach temperatures comparable to box cookers. Reflector cookers use various concentrating geometries (dish, trough, Fresnel mirrors) to focus light on a cooking container. These cookers reach temperatures of 315°C (599°F) and above but require direct light to function properly and must be repositioned to track the Sun.[\[41\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-41) ### Process heat Main articles: [Solar pond](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_pond "Solar pond"), [Salt evaporation pond](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salt_evaporation_pond "Salt evaporation pond"), and [Solar furnace](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_furnace "Solar furnace") Solar concentrating technologies such as parabolic dish, trough and Scheffler reflectors can provide process heat for commercial and industrial applications. The first commercial system was the [Solar Total Energy Project](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Total_Energy_Project "Solar Total Energy Project") (STEP) in Shenandoah, Georgia, US where a field of 114 parabolic dishes provided 50% of the process heating, air conditioning and electrical requirements for a clothing factory. This grid-connected cogeneration system provided 400kW of electricity plus thermal energy in the form of 401kW steam and 468kW chilled water and had a one-hour peak load thermal storage.[\[42\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-42) Evaporation ponds are shallow pools that concentrate dissolved solids through [evaporation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evaporation "Evaporation"). The use of evaporation ponds to obtain salt from seawater is one of the oldest applications of solar energy. Modern uses include concentrating brine solutions used in leach mining and removing dissolved solids from waste streams.[\[43\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-43) [Clothes lines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clothes_line "Clothes line"), [clotheshorses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clotheshorse "Clotheshorse"), and clothes racks dry clothes through evaporation by wind and sunlight without consuming electricity or gas. In some states of the United States legislation protects the "right to dry" clothes.[\[44\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-44) Unglazed transpired collectors (UTC) are perforated sun-facing walls used for preheating ventilation air. UTCs can raise the incoming air temperature up to 22°C (40°F) and deliver outlet temperatures of 45–60°C (113–140°F).[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-UTC-45) The short payback period of transpired collectors (3 to 12years) makes them a more cost-effective alternative than glazed collection systems.[\[45\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-UTC-45) As of 2003, over 80 systems with a combined collector area of 35,000 square metres (380,000sqft) had been installed worldwide, including an 860m2 (9,300sqft) collector in [Costa Rica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica") used for drying coffee beans and a 1,300m2 (14,000sqft) collector in [Coimbatore](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coimbatore "Coimbatore"), India, used for drying marigolds.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Leon_2006-46)\[ _[needs update](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_\] ### Water treatment Main articles: [Solar still](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_still "Solar still"), [Solar water disinfection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_water_disinfection "Solar water disinfection"), [Solar desalination](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_desalination "Solar desalination"), and [Solar Powered Desalination Unit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Powered_Desalination_Unit "Solar Powered Desalination Unit") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Indonesia-sodis-gross.jpg/500px-Indonesia-sodis-gross.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Indonesia-sodis-gross.jpg) [Solar water disinfection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_water_disinfection "Solar water disinfection") in Indonesia Solar distillation can be used to make [saline](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saline_water "Saline water") or [brackish water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brackish_water "Brackish water") potable. The first recorded instance of this was by 16th-century Arab alchemists.[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Tiwari_2003-47) A large-scale solar distillation project was first constructed in 1872 in the [Chilean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chile "Chile") mining town of Las Salinas.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Daniels_1964-48) The plant, which had solar collection area of 4,700m2 (51,000sqft), could produce up to 22,700L (5,000impgal; 6,000USgal) per day and operate for 40years.[\[48\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Daniels_1964-48) Individual [still](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Still "Still") designs include single-slope, double-slope (or greenhouse type), vertical, conical, inverted absorber, multi-wick, and multiple effect. These stills can operate in passive, active, or hybrid modes. Double-slope stills are the most economical for decentralized domestic purposes, while active multiple effect units are more suitable for large-scale applications.[\[47\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Tiwari_2003-47) Solar water [disinfection](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disinfection "Disinfection") (SODIS) involves exposing water-filled plastic [polyethylene terephthalate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polyethylene_terephthalate "Polyethylene terephthalate") (PET) bottles to sunlight for several hours.[\[49\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-49) Exposure times vary depending on weather and climate from a minimum of six hours to two days during fully overcast conditions.[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-SODIS_CDC-50) It is recommended by the [World Health Organization](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") as a viable method for household water treatment and safe storage.[\[51\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-51) Over two million people in developing countries use this method for their daily drinking water.[\[50\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-SODIS_CDC-50) Solar energy may be used in a water stabilization pond to treat [waste water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waste_water "Waste water") without chemicals or electricity. A further environmental advantage is that [algae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algae "Algae") grow in such ponds and consume [carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") in photosynthesis, although algae may produce toxic chemicals that make the water unusable.[\[52\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-52)[\[53\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-53) ### Molten salt technology Molten salt can be employed as a [thermal energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_energy_storage "Thermal energy storage") method to retain thermal energy collected by a [solar tower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power_tower "Solar power tower") or [solar trough](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_trough "Solar trough") of a [concentrated solar power plant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power_plant "Concentrated solar power plant") so that it can be used to generate electricity in bad weather or at night. It was demonstrated in the [Solar Two](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Two "Solar Two") project from 1995 to 1999. The system is predicted to have an annual efficiency of 99%, a reference to the energy retained by storing heat before turning it into electricity, versus converting heat directly into electricity.[\[54\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-54)[\[55\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-55)[\[56\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-56) The molten salt mixtures vary. The most extended mixture contains [sodium nitrate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sodium_nitrate "Sodium nitrate"), [potassium nitrate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potassium_nitrate "Potassium nitrate") and [calcium nitrate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcium_nitrate "Calcium nitrate"). It is non-flammable and non-toxic, and has already been used in the chemical and metals industries as a heat-transport fluid. Hence, experience with such systems exists in non-solar applications. The salt melts at 131°C (268°F). It is kept liquid at 288°C (550°F) in an insulated "cold" storage tank. The liquid salt is pumped through panels in a solar collector where the focused irradiance heats it to 566°C (1,051°F). It is then sent to a hot storage tank. This is so well insulated that the thermal energy can be usefully stored for up to a week.[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-57) When electricity is needed, the hot salt is pumped to a conventional steam-generator to produce [superheated steam](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Superheated_steam "Superheated steam") for a turbine/generator as used in any conventional coal, oil, or nuclear power plant. A 100-megawatt turbine would need a tank about 9.1 metres (30ft) tall and 24 metres (79ft) in diameter to drive it for four hours by this design. Several [parabolic trough](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parabolic_trough "Parabolic trough") power plants in Spain[\[58\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-58) and [solar power tower](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power_tower "Solar power tower") developer [SolarReserve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SolarReserve "SolarReserve") use this thermal energy storage concept. The [Solana Generating Station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solana_Generating_Station "Solana Generating Station") in the U.S. has six hours of storage by molten salt. In Chile, The Cerro Dominador power plant has a 110MW solar-thermal tower, the heat is transferred to [molten salts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_energy_storage#Molten_salt_technology "Thermal energy storage").[\[59\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-59) The molten salts then transfer their heat in a heat exchanger to water, generating superheated steam, which feeds a turbine that transforms the kinetic energy of the steam into electric energy using the [Rankine cycle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rankine_cycle "Rankine cycle").[\[60\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-60) In this way, the Cerro Dominador plant is capable of generating around 110 MW of power.[\[61\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-61) The plant has an advanced storage system enabling it to generate electricity for up to 17.5 hours without direct solar radiation, which allows it to provide a stable electricity supply without interruptions if required. The Project secured up to 950 GW·h per year sale. Another project is the María Elena plant[\[62\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-62) is a 400MW thermo-solar complex in the northern [Chilean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chile "Chile") region of [Antofagasta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antofagasta_Region "Antofagasta Region") employing molten salt technology. ## Electricity production [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Greencap_Energy_Solar_Panels_on_Airedale_Hospital.jpg/500px-Greencap_Energy_Solar_Panels_on_Airedale_Hospital.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Greencap_Energy_Solar_Panels_on_Airedale_Hospital.jpg) Greencap Energy solar array on [NHS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Health_Service "National Health Service") hospital in [Keighley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keighley "Keighley"), [England](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/England "England") These paragraphs are an excerpt from [Solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power").\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Solar_power&action=edit)\] [Solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power "Solar power"), also known as solar electricity, is the conversion of energy from [sunlight](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") into [electricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electricity "Electricity"), either directly using [photovoltaics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics") (PV) or indirectly using [concentrated solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power "Concentrated solar power"). [Solar panels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_panels "Solar panels") use the [photovoltaic effect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaic_effect "Photovoltaic effect") to convert light into an [electric current](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_current "Electric current").[\[63\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-63) Concentrated solar power systems use [lenses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lenses "Lenses") or mirrors and [solar tracking](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_tracking "Solar tracking") systems to focus a large area of sunlight to a hot spot, often to drive a [steam turbine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steam_turbine "Steam turbine"). Photovoltaics (PV) were initially solely used as a source of electricity for small and medium-sized applications, from the [calculator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calculator "Calculator") powered by a single solar cell to remote homes powered by an [off-grid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Off-grid "Off-grid") rooftop PV system. Commercial concentrated solar power plants were first developed in the 1980s. Since then, as the cost of solar panels has fallen, grid-connected [solar PV systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_PV_systems "Solar PV systems")' capacity and production have [doubled about every three years](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Growth_of_photovoltaics "Growth of photovoltaics"). Three-quarters of new generation capacity is solar,[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Solar_power_Gabb-64) with both millions of rooftop installations and gigawatt-scale [photovoltaic power stations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaic_power_station "Photovoltaic power station") continuing to be built. In 2024, solar power generated 7% of global electricity and over 1% of [primary energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primary_energy "Primary energy") (2.7% by the substitution method), adding twice as much new electricity as coal.[\[65\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-65)[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Solar_power_:0-66)[\[67\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-67) Along with onshore [wind power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power"), [utility-scale solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Utility-scale_solar "Utility-scale solar") is the source with the cheapest [levelised cost of electricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levelised_cost_of_electricity "Levelised cost of electricity") for new installations in most countries.[\[68\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-68)[\[69\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-69) [China](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power_in_China "Solar power in China") has about half the world’s solar power.[\[70\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-70) Almost half the solar power installed in 2022 was [mounted on rooftops](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rooftop_solar_power "Rooftop solar power").[\[71\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-71) Much more [low-carbon power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Low-carbon_power "Low-carbon power") is needed for [electrification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrification "Electrification") and to [limit climate change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation").[\[64\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Solar_power_Gabb-64) The [International Energy Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") said in 2022 that more effort was needed for grid integration and the mitigation of policy, regulation and financing challenges.[\[72\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-72) Nevertheless solar may greatly cut the cost of energy.[\[66\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Solar_power_:0-66) Solar is important for [energy security](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_security "Energy security").[\[73\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-73) ## Concentrated solar power Main article: [Concentrated solar power](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Concentrated_solar_power "Concentrated solar power") Concentrating Solar Power (CSP) systems use lenses or mirrors and tracking systems to focus a large area of sunlight into a small beam. The concentrated heat is then used as a heat source for a conventional power plant.[\[74\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-:1-74) A wide range of concentrating technologies exists; the most developed are the parabolic trough, the solar tower collectors, the concentrating linear Fresnel reflector, and the Stirling dish. Various techniques are used to track the Sun and focus light. In all of these systems, a [working fluid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_fluid "Working fluid") is heated by the concentrated sunlight, and is then used for power generation or energy storage.[\[74\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-:1-74)[\[75\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-75) Designs need to account for the risk of a [dust storm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dust_storm "Dust storm"), [hail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hail "Hail"), or another extreme weather event that can damage the fine glass surfaces of solar power plants. Metal grills would allow a high percentage of sunlight to enter the mirrors and solar panels while also preventing most damage. ## Architecture and urban planning Main articles: [Passive solar building design](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_solar_building_design "Passive solar building design") and [Urban heat island](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Technische_Universit%C3%A4t_Darmstadt_-_Solar_Decathlon_2007.jpg/500px-Technische_Universit%C3%A4t_Darmstadt_-_Solar_Decathlon_2007.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Technische_Universit%C3%A4t_Darmstadt_-_Solar_Decathlon_2007.jpg) [Darmstadt University of Technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darmstadt_University_of_Technology "Darmstadt University of Technology"), Germany, won the 2007 [Solar Decathlon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Decathlon "Solar Decathlon") in Washington, DC with this [passive house](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_house "Passive house") designed for humid and hot subtropical climate.[\[76\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-76) Sunlight has influenced building design since the beginning of architectural history.[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Schittich_2003-77) Advanced solar architecture and urban planning methods were first employed by the [Greeks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Chinese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feng_shui#Archaeology "Feng shui"), who oriented their buildings toward the south to provide light and warmth.[\[78\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-78) The common features of [passive solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Passive_solar "Passive solar") architecture are orientation relative to the Sun, compact proportion (a low surface area to volume ratio), selective shading (overhangs) and [thermal mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_mass_(Building) "Thermal mass (Building)").[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Schittich_2003-77) When these features are tailored to the local climate and environment, they can produce well-lit spaces that stay in a comfortable temperature range. [Socrates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Socrates "Socrates")' Megaron House is a classic example of passive solar design.[\[77\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Schittich_2003-77) The most recent approaches to solar design use computer modeling tying together [solar lighting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daylighting_(architecture) "Daylighting (architecture)"), [heating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_heating "Solar heating") and [ventilation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_air_conditioning "Solar air conditioning") systems in an integrated [solar design](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_design "Solar design") package.[\[79\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-79) Active solar equipment such as pumps, fans, and switchable windows can complement passive design and improve system performance. Urban heat islands (UHI) are metropolitan areas with higher temperatures than that of the surrounding environment. The higher temperatures result from increased absorption of solar energy by urban materials such as asphalt and concrete, which have lower [albedos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albedo "Albedo") and higher [heat capacities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_capacity "Heat capacity") than those in the natural environment. A straightforward method of counteracting the UHI effect is to paint buildings and roads white and to plant trees in the area. Using these methods, a hypothetical "cool communities" program in Los Angeles has projected that urban temperatures could be reduced by approximately 3°C at an estimated cost of US$1 billion, giving estimated total annual benefits of US$530 million from reduced air-conditioning costs and healthcare savings.[\[80\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-80) ## Agriculture and horticulture [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Westland_kassen.jpg/500px-Westland_kassen.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Westland_kassen.jpg) [Greenhouses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenhouses "Greenhouses") like these in the Westland municipality of the Netherlands grow vegetables, fruits and flowers. [Agriculture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") and [horticulture](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Horticulture "Horticulture") seek to optimize the capture of solar energy to optimize the productivity of plants. Techniques such as timed planting cycles, tailored row orientation, staggered heights between rows and the mixing of plant varieties can improve crop yields.[\[81\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-81)[\[82\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-82)[\[83\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-agrPV-83) While sunlight is generally considered a plentiful resource, the exceptions highlight the importance of solar energy to agriculture. During the short growing seasons of the [Little Ice Age](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age"), French and [English](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power_in_the_United_Kingdom "Solar power in the United Kingdom") farmers employed fruit walls to maximize the collection of solar energy. These walls acted as thermal masses and accelerated ripening by keeping plants warm. Early fruit walls were built perpendicular to the ground and facing south, but over time, sloping walls were developed to make better use of sunlight. In 1699, [Nicolas Fatio de Duillier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nicolas_Fatio_de_Duillier "Nicolas Fatio de Duillier") even suggested using a [tracking mechanism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_tracker "Solar tracker") which could pivot to follow the Sun.[\[84\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-84) Applications of solar energy in agriculture aside from growing crops include pumping water, drying crops, brooding chicks and drying chicken manure.[\[46\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Leon_2006-46)[\[85\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-85) More recently the technology has been embraced by [vintners](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winemakers "Winemakers"), who use the energy generated by solar panels to power grape presses.[\[86\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-86) [Greenhouses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse") convert solar light to heat, enabling year-round production and the growth (in enclosed environments) of specialty crops and other plants not naturally suited to the local climate. Primitive greenhouses were first used during Roman times to produce [cucumbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cucumbers "Cucumbers") year-round for the Roman emperor [Tiberius](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tiberius "Tiberius").[\[87\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-87) The first modern greenhouses were built in Europe in the 16th century to keep exotic plants brought back from explorations abroad.[\[88\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-88) Greenhouses remain an important part of horticulture today. Plastic transparent materials have also been used to similar effect in [polytunnels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polytunnel "Polytunnel") and [row covers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Row_cover "Row cover"). ## Transport Main articles: [Solar vehicle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_vehicle "Solar vehicle"), [Solar-charged vehicle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar-charged_vehicle "Solar-charged vehicle"), [Electric boat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_boat "Electric boat"), [Solar balloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_balloon "Solar balloon"), and [Solar aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_aircraft "Solar aircraft") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Nuna_7.jpg/500px-Nuna_7.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Nuna_7.jpg) [Winner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuna_7 "Nuna 7") of the 2013 [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") in Australia [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Flea_Hop_HB-SIA_-_Solar_Impulse.jpg/500px-Flea_Hop_HB-SIA_-_Solar_Impulse.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Flea_Hop_HB-SIA_-_Solar_Impulse.jpg) [Solar electric aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_aircraft "Solar aircraft") circumnavigating the globe in 2015 Development of a solar-powered car has been an engineering goal since the 1980s. The [World Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") is a biannual solar-powered car race, where teams from universities and enterprises compete over 3,021 kilometres (1,877mi) across central Australia from [Darwin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darwin,_Northern_Territory "Darwin, Northern Territory") to [Adelaide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide"). In 1987, when it was founded, the winner's average speed was 67 kilometres per hour (42mph) and by 2007 the winner's average speed had improved to 90.87 kilometres per hour (56.46mph).[\[89\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-89) The [North American Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_American_Solar_Challenge "North American Solar Challenge") and the planned [South African Solar Challenge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/South_African_Solar_Challenge "South African Solar Challenge") are comparable competitions that reflect an international interest in the engineering and development of solar powered vehicles.[\[90\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-90)[\[91\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-91) Some vehicles use solar panels for auxiliary power, such as for air conditioning, to keep the interior cool, thus reducing fuel consumption.[\[92\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-92)[\[93\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-93) In 1975, the first practical solar boat was constructed in England.[\[94\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-94) By 1995, passenger boats incorporating PV panels began appearing and are now used extensively.[\[95\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-95) In 1996, [Kenichi Horie](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kenichi_Horie "Kenichi Horie") made the first solar-powered crossing of the Pacific Ocean, and the _Sun21_ catamaran made the first solar-powered crossing of the Atlantic Ocean in the winter of 2006–2007.[\[96\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-96) There were plans to circumnavigate the globe in 2010.[\[97\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-97) In 1974, the unmanned [AstroFlight Sunrise](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AstroFlight_Sunrise "AstroFlight Sunrise") airplane made the first solar flight. On 29 April 1979, the _[Solar Riser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Riser "Solar Riser")_ made the first flight in a solar-powered, fully controlled, man-carrying flying machine, reaching an altitude of 40ft (12m). In 1980, the _[Gossamer Penguin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gossamer_Albatross#Solar-powered_variants "Gossamer Albatross")_ made the first piloted flights powered solely by photovoltaics. This was quickly followed by the _[Solar Challenger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Challenger "Solar Challenger")_ which crossed the English Channel in July 1981. In 1990 [Eric Scott Raymond](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eric_Scott_Raymond "Eric Scott Raymond") in 21 hops flew from California to North Carolina using solar power.[\[98\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-98) Developments then turned back to unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) with the _[Pathfinder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NASA_Pathfinder "NASA Pathfinder")_ (1997) and subsequent designs, culminating in the _[Helios](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Helios_Prototype "Helios Prototype")_ which set the altitude record for a non-rocket-propelled aircraft at 29,524 metres (96,864ft) in 2001.[\[99\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-99) The _[Zephyr](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QinetiQ_Zephyr "QinetiQ Zephyr")_, developed by [BAE Systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems"), is the latest in a line of record-breaking solar aircraft, making a 54-hour flight in 2007, and month-long flights were envisioned by 2010.[\[100\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-100) From March 2015 to July 2016, [Solar Impulse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Impulse "Solar Impulse"), an [electric aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_aircraft "Electric aircraft"), successfully circumnavigated the globe. It is a single-seat plane powered by [solar cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell") and capable of taking off under its own power. The design allows the aircraft to remain airborne for several days.[\[101\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-101) A [solar balloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_balloon "Solar balloon") is a black balloon that is filled with ordinary air. As sunlight shines on the balloon, the air inside is heated and expands, causing an upward [buoyancy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Buoyancy "Buoyancy") force, much like an artificially heated [hot air balloon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hot_air_balloon "Hot air balloon"). Some solar balloons are large enough for human flight, but usage is generally limited to the toy market as the surface-area to payload-weight ratio is relatively high.[\[102\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-102) ### Squad Solar vehicle [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Squad_Solar_Car_%28Fully_Charged_2022%29.jpg/500px-Squad_Solar_Car_%28Fully_Charged_2022%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Squad_Solar_Car_(Fully_Charged_2022).jpg) [Squad Solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Squad_Solar "Squad Solar") The [Squad Solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Squad_Solar "Squad Solar") is a [Neighborhood Electric Vehicle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neighborhood_Electric_Vehicle "Neighborhood Electric Vehicle") that has a solar roof and can be plugged into a normal 120 volt outlet to be charged.[\[103\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-103) ## Fuel production [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Photo_of_the_Week-_Boosting_Solar_Technology_%288722948189%29.jpg/500px-Photo_of_the_Week-_Boosting_Solar_Technology_%288722948189%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Photo_of_the_Week-_Boosting_Solar_Technology_(8722948189).jpg) Concentrated solar panels are getting a power boost. [Pacific Northwest National Laboratory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_National_Laboratory "Pacific Northwest National Laboratory") (PNNL) will be testing a new concentrated solar power system – one that can help natural gas power plants reduce their fuel usage by up to 20 percent.\[ _[needs update](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")_\] Main articles: [Solar chemical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_chemical "Solar chemical"), [Solar fuel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_fuel "Solar fuel"), and [Artificial photosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_photosynthesis "Artificial photosynthesis") Solar chemical processes use solar energy to drive chemical reactions. These processes offset energy that would otherwise come from a fossil fuel source and can also convert solar energy into storable and transportable fuels. Solar induced chemical reactions can be divided into thermochemical or [photochemical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photochemical "Photochemical").[\[104\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-104) A variety of fuels can be produced by [artificial photosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artificial_photosynthesis "Artificial photosynthesis").[\[105\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-105) The multielectron catalytic chemistry involved in making carbon-based fuels (such as [methanol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methanol "Methanol")) from reduction of [carbon dioxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") is challenging; a feasible alternative is [hydrogen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") production from protons, though use of water as the source of electrons (as plants do) requires mastering the multielectron oxidation of two water molecules to molecular oxygen.[\[106\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-106) Some have envisaged working solar fuel plants in coastal metropolitan areas by 2050– the splitting of seawater providing hydrogen to be run through adjacent fuel-cell electric power plants and the pure water by-product going directly into the municipal water system.[\[107\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-107) In addition, chemical energy storage is another solution to solar energy storage.[\[108\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-108) [Hydrogen production](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen_production "Hydrogen production") technologies have been a significant area of solar chemical research since the 1970s. Aside from electrolysis driven by photovoltaic or photochemical cells, several thermochemical processes have also been explored. One such route uses concentrators to split water into oxygen and hydrogen at high temperatures (2,300–2,600°C or 4,200–4,700°F).[\[109\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-109) Another approach uses the heat from solar concentrators to drive the [steam reformation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steam_reforming "Steam reforming") of natural gas thereby increasing the overall hydrogen yield compared to conventional reforming methods.[\[110\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-110) Thermochemical cycles characterized by the decomposition and regeneration of reactants present another avenue for hydrogen production. The Solzinc process under development at the [Weizmann Institute of Science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weizmann_Institute_of_Science "Weizmann Institute of Science") uses a 1MW solar furnace to decompose [zinc oxide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zinc_oxide "Zinc oxide") (ZnO) at temperatures above 1,200°C (2,200°F). This initial reaction produces pure zinc, which can subsequently be reacted with water to produce hydrogen.[\[111\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-111) ## Energy storage methods Main articles: [Energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_storage "Energy storage"), [Seasonal thermal energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seasonal_thermal_energy_storage "Seasonal thermal energy storage"), [Phase change material](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phase_change_material "Phase change material"), [Grid energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grid_energy_storage "Grid energy storage"), and [Vehicle-to-grid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vehicle-to-grid "Vehicle-to-grid") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Andasol_3.jpg/500px-Andasol_3.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Andasol_3.jpg) [Thermal energy storage](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_energy_storage "Thermal energy storage"). The [Andasol](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andasol_solar_power_station "Andasol solar power station") CSP plant uses tanks of molten salt to store solar energy. [Thermal mass](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_mass "Thermal mass") systems can store solar energy in the form of heat at domestically useful temperatures for daily or [interseasonal durations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seasonal_thermal_energy_storage "Seasonal thermal energy storage"). Thermal storage systems generally use readily available materials with high [specific heat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Specific_heat "Specific heat") capacities such as water, earth and stone. Well-designed systems can lower [peak demand](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peak_demand "Peak demand"), shift time-of-use to [off-peak](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/off-peak "wikt:off-peak") hours and reduce overall heating and cooling requirements.[\[112\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-112)[\[113\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-113) Phase change materials such as [paraffin wax](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paraffin_wax "Paraffin wax") and [Glauber's salt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sodium_sulfate#Thermal_storage "Sodium sulfate") are another thermal storage medium. These materials are inexpensive, readily available, and can deliver domestically useful temperatures (approximately 64°C or 147°F). The "Dover House" (in [Dover](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dover,_Massachusetts "Dover, Massachusetts"), Massachusetts) was the first to use a Glauber's salt heating system, in 1948.[\[114\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-114) Solar energy can also be stored at high temperatures using [molten salts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molten_salt "Molten salt"). Salts are an effective storage medium because they are low-cost, have a high specific heat capacity, and can deliver heat at temperatures compatible with conventional power systems. The [Solar Two](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Solar_Project#Solar_Two "The Solar Project") project used this method of energy storage, allowing it to store 1.44 [terajoules](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terajoule "Terajoule") (400,000kWh) in its 68 m3 storage tank with an annual storage efficiency of about 99%.[\[115\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-115) Off-grid [PV systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics") have traditionally used [rechargeable batteries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rechargeable_batteries "Rechargeable batteries") to store excess electricity. With grid-tied systems, excess electricity can be sent to the transmission [grid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grid-tied_electrical_system "Grid-tied electrical system"), while standard grid electricity can be used to meet shortfalls. [Net metering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_metering "Net metering") programs give household systems credit for any electricity they deliver to the grid. This is handled by 'rolling back' the meter whenever the home produces more electricity than it consumes. If the net electricity use is below zero, the utility then rolls over the kilowatt-hour credit to the next month.[\[116\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-116) Other approaches involve the use of two meters, to measure electricity consumed vs. electricity produced. This is less common due to the increased installation cost of the second meter. Most standard meters accurately measure in both directions, making a second meter unnecessary. [Pumped-storage hydroelectricity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pumped-storage_hydroelectricity "Pumped-storage hydroelectricity") stores energy in the form of water pumped when energy is available from a lower elevation reservoir to a higher elevation one. The energy is recovered when demand is high by releasing the water, with the pump becoming a hydroelectric power generator.[\[117\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-117) ## Development, deployment and economics [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/03242012Taller_sostenibilidad_lore060.jpg/500px-03242012Taller_sostenibilidad_lore060.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:03242012Taller_sostenibilidad_lore060.jpg) Participants in a workshop on sustainable development inspect solar panels at [Monterrey Institute of Technology and Higher Education, Mexico City](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monterrey_Institute_of_Technology_and_Higher_Education,_Mexico_City "Monterrey Institute of Technology and Higher Education, Mexico City") on top of a building on campus. Further information: [Deployment of solar power to energy grids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deployment_of_solar_power_to_energy_grids "Deployment of solar power to energy grids") See also: [Cost of electricity by source](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cost_of_electricity_by_source "Cost of electricity by source") and [Renewable energy by country](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renewable_energy_by_country "Renewable energy by country") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Solar_PV_module_prices.svg/500px-Solar_PV_module_prices.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_PV_module_prices.svg) Cost development of solar PV modules per watt Beginning with the surge in [coal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coal "Coal") use, which accompanied the [Industrial Revolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), energy consumption steadily transitioned from wood and biomass to [fossil fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"). The early development of solar technologies starting in the 1860s was driven by an expectation that coal would soon become scarce. However, development of solar technologies stagnated in the early 20th century in the face of the increasing availability, economy, and utility of coal and [petroleum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum").[\[118\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-118) The [1973 oil embargo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") and [1979 energy crisis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1979_energy_crisis "1979 energy crisis") caused a reorganization of energy policies around the world. It brought renewed attention to developing solar technologies.[\[119\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-119)[\[120\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-120) Deployment strategies focused on incentive programs such as the Federal Photovoltaic Utilization Program in the US and the Sunshine Program in Japan. Other efforts included the formation of research facilities in the US (SERI, now [NREL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NREL "NREL")), Japan ( [NEDO](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Energy_and_Industrial_Technology_Development_Organization "New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization")), and [Germany](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power_in_Germany "Solar power in Germany") ( [Fraunhofer Institute for Solar Energy Systems ISE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fraunhofer_Society "Fraunhofer Society")).[\[121\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-121) Commercial solar water heaters began appearing in the United States in the 1890s.[\[122\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-122) These systems saw increasing use until the 1920s but were gradually replaced by cheaper and more reliable heating fuels.[\[123\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-123) As with photovoltaics, [solar water heating](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_water_heating "Solar water heating") attracted renewed attention as a result of the oil crises in the 1970s, but interest subsided in the 1980s due to falling petroleum prices. Development in the solar water heating sector progressed steadily throughout the 1990s, and annual growth rates have averaged 20% since 1999.[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-SWH_2008-124) Although generally underestimated, solar water heating and cooling is by far the most widely deployed solar technology with an estimated capacity of 154 GW as of 2007.[\[124\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-SWH_2008-124) The [International Energy Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") has said that solar energy can make considerable contributions to solving some of the most urgent problems the world now faces:[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ie11-1) > The development of affordable, inexhaustible, and clean solar energy technologies will have huge longer-term benefits. It will increase countries' energy security through reliance on an indigenous, inexhaustible, and mostly import-independent resource, enhance sustainability, reduce pollution, lower the costs of mitigating climate change, and keep fossil fuel prices lower than otherwise. These advantages are global. Hence the additional costs of the incentives for early deployment should be considered learning investments; they must be wisely spent and need to be widely shared.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ie11-1) In 2011, a report by the [International Energy Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/International_Energy_Agency "International Energy Agency") found that solar energy technologies such as photovoltaics, solar hot water, and concentrated solar power could provide a third of the world's energy by 2060 if politicians commit to limiting [climate change](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and [transitioning to renewable energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_transition "Energy transition"). The energy from the Sun could play a key role in de-carbonizing the global economy alongside improvements in [energy efficiency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use") and imposing costs on [greenhouse gas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas") emitters. "The strength of solar is the incredible variety and flexibility of applications, from small scale to big scale".[\[125\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-125) > We have proved ... that after our stores of oil and coal are exhausted the human race can receive unlimited power from the rays of the Sun. — [Frank Shuman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frank_Shuman "Frank Shuman"), _The New York Times_, 2 July 1916.[\[26\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-nytimes.com-26) In 2021 [Lazard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lazard "Lazard") estimated the [levelized cost](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levelized_cost_of_energy "Levelized cost of energy") of new build unsubsidized utility scale solar electricity at less than 37 dollars per MWh and existing coal-fired power above that amount.[\[126\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-auto-126)[\[127\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-:0-127) The 2021 report also said that new solar was also cheaper than new gas-fired power, but not generally existing gas power.[\[127\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-:0-127) ### Emerging technologies See also: [Thin-film solar cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thin-film_solar_cell "Thin-film solar cell") [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Best_Research-Cell_Efficiencies.svg/500px-Best_Research-Cell_Efficiencies.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Best_Research-Cell_Efficiencies.svg) [Solar cell efficiencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell_efficiencies "Solar cell efficiencies") of various cell technologies (including both [single-crystal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single-crystal_silicon "Single-crystal silicon") and [thin film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thin-film_solar_cell "Thin-film solar cell") technologies) as tracked by [NREL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NREL "NREL") #### Experimental solar power Main article: [Solar power §Emerging technologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power#Emerging_technologies "Solar power") Concentrated photovoltaics (CPV) systems employ sunlight concentrated onto photovoltaic surfaces for the purpose of electricity generation. [Thermoelectric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermogenerator "Thermogenerator"), or "thermovoltaic" devices convert a temperature difference between dissimilar materials into an electric current. #### Floating solar arrays This section is an excerpt from [Floating solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floating_solar "Floating solar").\[ [edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Floating_solar&action=edit)\] [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Farniente2.jpg/500px-Farniente2.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Farniente2.jpg) Floating photovoltaic on an irrigation pond [Floating solar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floating_solar "Floating solar") or floating photovoltaics (FPV), sometimes called floatovoltaics, are [solar panels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell_panel "Solar cell panel") mounted on a structure that floats. The structures that hold the panels usually consist of plastic buoys and cables. They are then placed on a body of water (e.g., Reservoirs, quarry lakes, irrigation canals or remediation and tailing ponds).[\[128\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-128)[\[129\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-129)[\[130\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-130)[\[131\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-131)[\[132\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-132) The systems can have advantages over [photovoltaics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaics "Photovoltaics") (PV) on land. Water surfaces may be less expensive than the cost of land, and there are fewer rules and regulations for structures built on bodies of water not used for recreation. [Life cycle analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Life-cycle_assessment "Life-cycle assessment") indicates that foam-based FPV[\[133\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Floating_solar_:3-133) have some of the shortest [energy payback times](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_energy_gain "Net energy gain") (1.3 years) and the lowest [greenhouse gas emissions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") to energy ratio (11kg CO2 eq/MWh) in crystalline silicon solar photovoltaic technologies reported.[\[134\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-134) FPV can be used to generate electricity for any application as well as to make [green hydrogen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Green_hydrogen "Green hydrogen") via electrolysis on the same water they are floating on. Floating arrays can achieve higher efficiencies than PV panels on land because water cools the panels. The panels can have a special coating to prevent rust or corrosion.[\[135\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-135) Floating SPV also provide shade, slow evaporation and inhibit the growth of algae.[\[136\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-136) The market for this [renewable energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") technology has grown rapidly since 2016. The first 20 plants with capacities of a few dozen [kWp](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nominal_power_(photovoltaic)#Watt-peak "Nominal power (photovoltaic)") were built between 2007 and 2013.[\[137\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Floating_solar_:0-137) Installed power grew from 3 GW in 2020, to 13 GW in 2022,[\[138\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Floating_solar_:7-138) surpassing a prediction of 10 GW by 2025.[\[139\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-139) The World Bank estimated there are 6,600 large bodies of water suitable for floating solar, with a technical capacity of over 4,000 GW if 10% of their surfaces were covered with panels.[\[138\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Floating_solar_:7-138) The U.S. has more floating solar potential than any other country in the world.[\[140\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Floating_solar_:9-140) Bodies of water suitable for floating solar are well-distributed throughout the U.S. The southeast and southern U.S. plains states generally have reservoirs with the largest capacities.[\[140\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Floating_solar_:9-140) #### Solar-assisted heat pump Main article: [Solar-assisted heat pump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar-assisted_heat_pump "Solar-assisted heat pump") A [heat pump](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_pump "Heat pump") is a device that provides heat energy from a source of heat to a destination called a "heat sink". Heat pumps are designed to move [thermal energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thermal_energy "Thermal energy") opposite to the direction of spontaneous heat flow by absorbing heat from a cold space and releasing it to a warmer one. A solar-assisted heat pump represents the integration of a heat pump and [thermal solar panels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_thermal_collector "Solar thermal collector") in a single integrated system. Typically these two technologies are used separately (or only placing them in parallel) to produce [hot water](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_heating "Water heating").[\[141\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ref1-141) In this system the solar thermal panel performs the function of the low temperature heat source and the heat produced is used to feed the heat pump's evaporator.[\[142\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-ref2-142) The goal of this system is to get high [COP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coefficient_of_performance "Coefficient of performance") and then produce energy in a more [efficient](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy_conversion_efficiency "Energy conversion efficiency") and less expensive way. It is possible to use any type of solar thermal panel (sheet and tubes, roll-bond, heat pipe, thermal plates) or [hybrid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photovoltaic_thermal_hybrid_solar_collector "Photovoltaic thermal hybrid solar collector") ( [mono](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mono-crystalline_silicon "Mono-crystalline silicon")/ [polycrystalline](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polycrystalline_silicon "Polycrystalline silicon"), [thin film](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thin-film_solar_cell "Thin-film solar cell")) in combination with the heat pump. The use of a hybrid panel is preferable because it allows covering a part of the electricity demand of the heat pump and reduces the power consumption and consequently the [variable costs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variable_cost "Variable cost") of the system. #### Solar aircraft [![](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Solar_Impulse_SI2_pilote_Bertrand_Piccard_Payerne_November_2014.jpg/500px-Solar_Impulse_SI2_pilote_Bertrand_Piccard_Payerne_November_2014.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Solar_Impulse_SI2_pilote_Bertrand_Piccard_Payerne_November_2014.jpg) In 2016, _[Solar Impulse 2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Impulse_2 "Solar Impulse 2")_ was the first [solar-powered aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar-powered_aircraft "Solar-powered aircraft") to complete a [circumnavigation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circumnavigation "Circumnavigation") of the world. See also: [Solar Impulse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Impulse "Solar Impulse") An [electric aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_aircraft "Electric aircraft") is an aircraft that runs on [electric motors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_motor "Electric motor") rather than [internal combustion engines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), with electricity coming from [fuel cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell"), [solar cells](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell"), [ultracapacitors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ultracapacitors "Ultracapacitors"), [power beaming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_beaming "Power beaming"),[\[143\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-143) or [batteries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_battery "Electric battery"). Currently, flying manned electric aircraft are mostly experimental demonstrators, though many small [unmanned aerial vehicles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle") are powered by batteries. [Electrically powered model aircraft](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Model_aircraft#Electric_power "Model aircraft") have been flown since the 1970s, with one report in 1957.[\[144\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Noth-144)[\[145\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-145) The first man-carrying electrically powered flights were made in 1973.[\[146\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-JAWA74-146) Between 2015 and 2016, a manned, solar-powered plane, _[Solar Impulse 2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_Impulse_2 "Solar Impulse 2")_, completed a circumnavigation of the Earth.[\[147\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_energy#cite_note-Batrawy-147) ## See also -
